target.title: feat(routing): support per-agent dmScope override
target.body:
## Summary

- Add optional `dmScope` field to `AgentConfig`, allowing each agent to override the global `session.dmScope`
- When set, the per-agent `dmScope` takes precedence over `session.dmScope` during session key resolution
- Falls back to the global `session.dmScope` when no per-agent override is configured

### Use case

When running multiple agents with different isolation needs:
- **Personal agent** (`dmScope: "main"`): all DMs collapse to a single shared session
- **DM agent** (inherits global `per-channel-peer`): each user gets an isolated session
- **Group agent**: unaffected by dmScope (groups always get their own session key)

```jsonc
{
  "session": { "dmScope": "per-channel-peer" },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "main", "dmScope": "main" },
      { "id": "dm" },
      { "id": "group" }
    ]
  }
}
```

### Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.agents.ts` | Add `dmScope?: DmScope` to `AgentConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add zod validation for `dmScope` |
| `src/routing/resolve-route.ts` | Look up per-agent `dmScope` in `choose()` |
| `src/config/schema.help.ts` | Add help text |
| `src/config/schema.labels.ts` | Add label |
| `src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts` | Add test for per-agent override |

## Test plan

- [x] `npx vitest run src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts` — all 42 tests pass
- [ ] Manual: configure two agents with different `dmScope` values, verify session keys

target.title: feat(feishu): extract option, options, form_value from card action callbacks
target.body:
## Problem

When users interact with Feishu interactive card components like `select_static` (dropdowns), `checkbox` (multi-select), or `form` (form submissions), the selected values are lost because `handleFeishuCardAction` only extracts data from `action.value`.

Feishu sends selection data in separate fields:
- `action.option` — for `select_static` single selection
- `action.options` — for `checkbox` / `multi_select` multiple selections  
- `action.form_value` — for `form` submission data

These fields are completely ignored by the current implementation, making it impossible for AI Agents to know what the user actually selected.

Related issue: #42754

## Solution

1. **Extended `FeishuCardActionEvent` type** to include `option`, `options`, and `form_value` fields
2. **Updated content extraction logic** to merge these fields with `action.value` when present, using priority: `form_value` > `options` > `option` > existing behavior
3. **Maintained full backward compatibility** — existing button interactions (`value.text`, `value.command`) work exactly as before

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/card-action.ts` — Type definition + extraction logic (14 lines changed)
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot.card-action.test.ts` — 4 new test cases (90 lines added)

## Test Plan

- [x] `select_static` with `option` field — value merged into JSON output
- [x] `checkbox` with `options` array — values merged into JSON output  
- [x] `form` with `form_value` object — values merged into JSON output
- [x] Existing button with `value.command` — backward compatible, unchanged behavior
- [x] All 6 tests pass (`vitest`)
- [x] TypeScript compilation passes

## AI Disclosure

This PR was authored with assistance from an AI coding assistant (Claude/Cursor). All code changes have been reviewed and tested.

target.title: fix(agents): classify compound read-only gateway actions correctly

- Problem: Compound gateway action names like `config.schema.lookup` are classified as mutating because `isMutatingToolCall` only checks the full action string against `READ_ONLY_ACTIONS`, and compound names are never in that set.
- Why it matters: Read-only gateway operations produce false "mutating action" classifications, which trigger unnecessary user-facing error notifications on tool failures.
- What changed: Added `lookup` to `READ_ONLY_ACTIONS` and updated the gateway/cron/canvas branch to extract the leaf verb from compound action names before checking the set.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The `_actions`-suffixed and `nodes` branches are left as-is since they do not use compound naming.

target.title: fix(infra): derive home directory from PREFIX on Android/Termux
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: On Android/Termux, `os.homedir()` reads `/etc/passwd` and returns `/home` which does not exist. OpenClaw crashes at startup because it cannot create its config directory.
- Why it matters: Termux is the primary way to run Node on Android. Without this fix, OpenClaw is unusable there unless the user manually sets HOME.
- What changed: Added a `resolveTermuxHome()` helper that derives `/data/data/com.termux/files/home` from the PREFIX env var. Wired it into both the main fallback chain and the tilde-expansion chain for OPENCLAW_HOME.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to non-Android platforms. HOME/USERPROFILE still take priority when set.

OpenClaw now starts correctly on Termux without requiring the user to manually export HOME.

target.title: fix(reply): suppress entire message when NO_REPLY appears on its own line
target.body:
## Problem

When the model outputs internal narration/reasoning followed by `NO_REPLY` on a separate line, the preceding text leaks to the user as a visible message.

```
Model output:    "Checked inbox, nothing new.\n\nNO_REPLY"
User sees:       "Checked inbox, nothing new."
Expected:        (nothing — the model intended silence)
```

This is especially common with heartbeat tasks and weaker models that output internal status summaries before appending `NO_REPLY`, but it also affects stronger models (Claude Opus 4.6) during heartbeat runs.

The current `stripSilentToken` removes the trailing `NO_REPLY` but delivers the preceding text. This violates the model's intent — when `NO_REPLY` appears on its own line, the preceding text is internal narration, not user-facing content.

## Fix

Introduces `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` in `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` which checks whether `NO_REPLY` appears on its own line (preceded by `\n` or at the start of text). When it does, the **entire** response is treated as silent.

Inline occurrences like `"😄 NO_REPLY"` continue to use `stripSilentToken` and deliver the non-token text, preserving the existing behavior from #30916.

### Decision matrix

| Pattern | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| `"NO_REPLY"` (exact) | ✅ Silent | ✅ Silent (unchanged) |
| `"😄 NO_REPLY"` (inline) | Delivers `😄` | Delivers `😄` (unchanged) |
| `"text...\n\nNO_REPLY"` (own line) | ❌ Delivers `text...` | ✅ Silent |
| `"tasks:\n• ok\nNO_REPLY"` (own line) | ❌ Delivers `tasks: • ok` | ✅ Silent |
| `"NO_REPLY -- nope"` (leading) | Delivers as-is | Delivers as-is (unchanged) |

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts`: Add `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/normalize-reply.ts`: Use own-line check before falling back to `stripSilentToken`
- `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts`: Add tests for `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`: Update `normalizeReplyPayload` tests

**+99 / -15 lines, 4 files.**

Closes #42472
Refs #30916, #33544, #27959, #30955

target.title: feat(daemon): auto-detect Node.js heap limit; add gateway.nodeMaxOldSpaceMb config
target.body:
## Problem

Node.js defaults to a ~1.5 GB V8 heap ceiling regardless of available system RAM. On machines with 8–128 GB this leaves most memory unused and causes hard OOM aborts (`CheckIneffectiveMarkCompact` → `FatalProcessOutOfMemory` → SIGABRT) under heavy parallel agent workloads. The issue also affects child Node processes spawned by the gateway, which inherit no heap configuration at all.

## Solution

At gateway install time, automatically compute a sane heap limit (85% of `os.totalmem()`) and inject it into `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=N` in the service environment. This propagates to child Node processes automatically via environment inheritance.

Users who want a specific value can set `gateway.nodeMaxOldSpaceMb` in their config. The interactive onboarding wizard shows the auto-detected value and lets them override it.

## Changes

- **`src/config/types.gateway.ts`** — adds `nodeMaxOldSpaceMb?: number` to `GatewayConfig`
- **`src/config/zod-schema.ts`** — schema: `z.number().int().min(256).optional()`
- **`src/config/schema.labels.ts`** / **`schema.help.ts`** — label + help text following existing conventions
- **`src/daemon/service-env.ts`** — adds `resolveDefaultNodeMaxOldSpaceMb()` and `resolveNodeOptions()` (both exported for testing); injects `NODE_OPTIONS` in `buildCommonServiceEnvironment`; adds `nodeMaxOldSpaceMb?` param to `buildServiceEnvironment`
- **`src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.ts`** — threads `config?.gateway?.nodeMaxOldSpaceMb` into `buildServiceEnvironment`
- **`src/wizard/onboarding.gateway-config.ts`** — adds heap prompt to non-quickstart flow, showing auto-detected value as hint, blank = auto-detect
- **`src/daemon/service-env.test.ts`** — 10 new tests covering auto-detect math, env override precedence, flag composition, `buildServiceEnvironment` integration

## Behaviour details

| Scenario | Result |
|---|---|
| No config, no `NODE_OPTIONS` | `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=<85% of RAM>` |
| `gateway.nodeMaxOldSpaceMb: 6144` set | `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144` |
| User already has `--max-old-space-size` in `NODE_OPTIONS` | Left unchanged (no override) |
| User has other flags in `NODE_OPTIONS` | Heap flag appended: `<existing> --max-old-space-size=<N>` |
| Quickstart flow | Auto-detect applied silently, no prompt shown |

## Testing

```
pnpm vitest run src/daemon/service-env.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
```

59 + 32 + 20 = 111 tests, all passing.

target.title: fix(slack): respect userTokenReadOnly flag for read operations
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `getTokenForOperation("read")` unconditionally returns `userToken ?? botToken`, ignoring the `userTokenReadOnly` configuration flag.
- Why it matters: When `userTokenReadOnly: false` is set (to enable user-token writes), read operations still prefer the user token even when it lacks broad read scopes like `channels:history`, causing API failures.
- What changed: Read operations now check `userTokenReadOnly` — when `false`, reads prefer `botToken` (which typically has full read scopes) and fall back to `userToken` only when bot token is unavailable. When `true` (default), behavior is unchanged (user token preferred for reads).
- What did NOT change: Write-path token selection logic, token resolution in `resolveSlackAccount`, Zod schema defaults, or any other Slack action behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42752

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When `userTokenReadOnly: false`, Slack read operations (readMessages, listPins, reactions, memberInfo, emojiList) now prefer the bot token instead of the user token. This prevents failures when the user token has limited read scopes (e.g., only `identify` and `search:read`).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — same tokens, different selection priority
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps
1. Configure Slack with both `botToken` (full scopes) and `userToken` (limited scopes: `identify`, `search:read`)
2. Set `userTokenReadOnly: false`
3. Try `readMessages` action

### Expected
- Reads use bot token (which has `channels:history`)

### Actual (before fix)
- Reads always use user token first → fails with scope error because user token lacks `channels:history`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- New tests added:
  - `uses bot token for reads when userTokenReadOnly is false`
  - `falls back to user token for reads when userTokenReadOnly is false and no bot token`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: userTokenReadOnly=true (default, unchanged), userTokenReadOnly=false (new behavior), no userToken (unchanged), no botToken with userTokenReadOnly=false (fallback)
- Edge cases checked: both tokens missing, only userToken present, only botToken present
- What you did **not** verify: live Slack API calls with real tokens

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — default behavior (`userTokenReadOnly: true`) is unchanged
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert single commit, or set `userTokenReadOnly: true` (default)
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/tools/slack-actions.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Read operations failing when `userTokenReadOnly: false` with both tokens configured

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Users who set `userTokenReadOnly: false` and relied on user token for reads may see different token used
  - Mitigation: Bot token has strictly broader scopes than user token in standard Slack setups; fallback to user token preserved when bot token is missing

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `userTokenReadOnly: true` + both tokens | userToken for reads | userToken for reads (unchanged) |
| `userTokenReadOnly: false` + both tokens | userToken for reads | **botToken for reads** |
| `userTokenReadOnly: false` + userToken only | userToken for reads | userToken for reads (fallback) |
| `userTokenReadOnly: true` + botToken only | botToken for reads | botToken for reads (unchanged) |

target.title: feat(feishu): expose reaction tool
target.body:
# PR 2 draft: Feishu reaction tool registration

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): expose reaction tool
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_reaction` tool with `add`, `remove`, and `list` actions.
- Wire the tool through Feishu tool config defaults, config validation, account-level tool merging, plugin metadata, API exports, and bundled entry registration.
- Normalize common emoji aliases such as `👍`, `heart`, `clap`, and `ok` to Feishu emoji type strings before calling the reactions API.
- Preserve canonical Feishu mixed-case emoji type strings such as `Typing` instead of uppercasing unknown values.
- Honor the existing `actions.reactions` policy gate as well as the new `tools.reactions` tool-registration gate.
- Document the Feishu-native reaction tool and `channels.feishu.tools.reactions` gate.

## Related upstream work

- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/39567
- Addresses the missing reaction-tool portion of https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/8692
- Complements the lower-level reaction bug fixes in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478
- Keeps scope narrow and does not change the existing generic `message(action="react")` path, reducing overlap with https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/34528 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Notes for reviewers

- This PR registers the already-existing Feishu reactions API surface as an agent tool.
- `tools.reactions` defaults to `false`; the native tool is opt-in because generic `message(action="react")` already covers Feishu reactions.
- `actions.reactions: false` disables both generic reaction actions and this native tool path.
- `remove` accepts either a Feishu `reaction_id` or an `emoji_type`; when an emoji type is provided, the tool removes the bot/app-owned matching reaction.
- Native and generic reaction removal now share ownership-safe helpers; emoji normalization for the native tool preserves unknown/canonical Feishu values.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can use the native `feishu_reaction` tool to add, list, and remove emoji reactions on real Feishu messages instead of having the reactions API implemented but unreachable from agents.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw checkout on this PR branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scope `im:message:reactions:write_only` enabled.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Loaded the PR branch locally.
2. Read the existing Feishu bot config from `C:\Users\Clark\.openclaw\openclaw.json` without printing secrets.
3. Enabled `channels.feishu.tools.reactions: true` and `channels.feishu.actions.reactions: true` in memory for the live run.
4. Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` to import `extensions/feishu/src/reaction-tool.ts` from this branch, call `registerFeishuReactionTools(...)`, resolve the registered `feishu_reaction` tool, and execute `tool.execute(...)` against the live Feishu API.
5. Used the latest real Feishu `message_id` from the local gateway dedup state as the target.
6. Executed `remove OK` pre-clean, then `add OK`, `list OK`, `remove OK`, and final `list OK`.

Evidence after fix:
Copied terminal output from the live command. IDs are redacted; no app secret/token is included.

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "proof": "feishu_reaction add/list/remove live API proof",
  "source": "local OpenClaw PR branch tool.execute against Feishu API",
  "selectedMessage": {
    "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
    "dedupSeenAtMs": 1779090392068
  },
  "account": {
    "domain": "feishu",
    "appId": "cli_aa...2dcce",
    "toolsReactions": true,
    "actionsReactions": true
  },
  "assertions": {
    "ownAfterAdd": 1,
    "ownAfterRemove": 0
  },
  "transcript": [
    {
      "label": "pre-clean remove OK",
      "params": {
        "action": "remove",
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK"
      },
      "output": {
        "success": true,
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK",
        "removed": false
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "add OK",
      "params": {
        "action": "add",
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK"
      },
      "output": {
        "success": true,
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK",
        "reaction_id": "XA3j_0...ZGUdx"
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "list OK after add",
      "params": {
        "action": "list",
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK"
      },
      "output": {
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "reactions": [
          {
            "reactionId": "XA3j_0...ZGUdx",
            "emojiType": "OK",
            "operatorType": "app",
            "operatorId": "cli_aa...2dcce"
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "remove OK",
      "params": {
        "action": "remove",
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK"
      },
      "output": {
        "success": true,
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK",
        "removed": {
          "reaction_id": "XA3j_0...ZGUdx"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "list OK after remove",
      "params": {
        "action": "list",
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "emoji_type": "OK"
      },
      "output": {
        "message_id": "om_x10...da556",
        "reactions": []
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: `add` returned a live `reaction_id`; `list` immediately returned one app-owned `OK` reaction whose `operatorId` matched the redacted app id; `remove` deleted that reaction; the final `list` returned an empty reaction list for `OK`.

What was not tested: visual screen recording of the Feishu chat UI; the proof above is copied terminal output from the real Feishu bot environment.

## Testing

```text
.\node_modules\.bin\vitest.cmd run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/reaction-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/tools-config.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/docx.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/drive.test.ts
```

target.title: perf(cli): speed up onboarding help startup
target.body:
## Summary
- lazy-load setup/onboard/configure action runtime so command help construction stays light
- add a scoped run-main help fast path for setup/onboard/configure without changing help output
- preserve dynamic provider auth flags in `onboard --help`
- add cold-import and run-main coverage for these help paths

## Verification
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm test src/cli/help-cold-imports.test.ts src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts src/cli/run-main.test.ts -- --reporter=default`
- `pnpm test src/commands/configure.commands.test.ts src/commands/onboard*.test.ts src/commands/setup*.test.ts -- --reporter=default`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- exact help-output diff vs `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_CLI_STARTUP_HELP_FAST_PATH=1`: no diff for setup/configure/onboard

## Benchmark
Command:
`pnpm test:startup:bench -- --case configureHelp --case onboardHelp --case setupHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --json --output .artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-after.json`

Before artifact: `.artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-check.json`
After artifact: `.artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-after.json`

| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |

| `setup --help` | 438.8ms | 443.8ms | 327.9MB | 206.4ms | 209.7ms | 239.9MB | -52.9% | -88.0MB |

target.body:
## Summary

- 新增 CapitalHftService（群益證券）OpenClaw 整合：HFT 報價訂閱、下單管線、海外期貨（SKOSQuoteLib）與股票支援
- 新增完整策略引擎：30+ 交易策略（均線交叉、布林通道、RSI、MACD、Ichimoku、SMC、網格交易、選擇權策略等）
- 新增套利引擎：跨交易所套利、期現套利、三角套利、日曆價差、Funding Rate 套利、配對交易
- 新增風險管理系統：RiskController、CorrelationMonitor、StopLossManager、PositionSizer、FillTracker
- 新增 HFT 引擎：MarketMaking、MeanReversion、OrderImbalance、TickMomentum、TWAP/VWAP 執行器
- 新增即時風控監控儀表板（DashboardServer + live-risk-monitor）
- 新增回測框架：Backtester、WalkForward、SensitivityAnalyzer、PortfolioBacktester
- 新增 Markowitz 投資組合最佳化、新聞情緒分析策略、IB adapter 與合約規格

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: HFT 報價觸發器在 paper-only 安全模式下正常運作，策略引擎模組可正常載入，風控檢查通過。所有真實下單管線已封鎖。

**Real environment tested**: Windows 11, Node.js v24.14.1, pnpm 10.x, OpenClaw v2026.5.5 本機開發環境, CapitalHftService 連接群益 SKQuoteLib (paper-only 模式)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. 執行 `pnpm brokerdesk:paper-hft:trigger` 觸發 HFT 報價事件
2. 執行 `pnpm capital:service-status` 確認服務安全狀態
3. 執行 `pnpm brokerdesk:paper-hft:check` 健康檢查
4. 執行 `pnpm check:openclaw-controlled-task-runner` 自動化檢查

**Evidence after fix**:
```
$ pnpm brokerdesk:paper-hft:trigger
→ quote.ageSeconds: 2410
→ bidAskUsable: true, bid: 15487.00, ask: 15488.00
→ risk.allowLiveTrading: false, writeBrokerOrders: false
→ lastStatus: "burst_executed"
→ reason: "Quote callback is new but not fresh/actionable for HFT paper burst."

$ pnpm capital:service-status
→ safety.sentOrder: false
→ safety.allowLiveTrading: false
→ safety.paperOnlyMode: true
→ safety.realOrderAllowed: false

$ pnpm brokerdesk:paper-hft:check
→ CAPITAL_PAPER_HFT_READINESS_CHECK=OK

$ pnpm check:openclaw-controlled-task-runner
→ OPENCLAW_CONTROLLED_TASK_RUNNER_CHECK=OK
```

**Observed result after fix**: 所有安全防護正常運作：paper-only 模式啟用、真實下單封鎖 (sentOrder=false, allowLiveTrading=false, realOrderAllowed=false)、報價觸發器正確辨識報價新鮮度、健康檢查全部通過 (READINESS_CHECK=OK, TASK_RUNNER_CHECK=OK)。

**What was not tested**:
- 策略引擎 `test-p0-risk.mjs` 等整合測試需安裝 `technicalindicators` 依賴後執行
- IB adapter 需連接 TWS Gateway 才能完整測試
- Dashboard 即時數據流需 CapitalHftService PID 存活

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm brokerdesk:paper-hft:check` → OK
- [x] `pnpm check:openclaw-controlled-task-runner` → OK
- [x] `pnpm brokerdesk:paper-hft:trigger` paper-only 安全模式驗證
- [x] `pnpm capital:service-status` 狀態報告完整
- [ ] `node scripts/test-p0-risk.mjs` P0 風控測試（需 technicalindicators）
- [ ] `node scripts/test-p1p2-modules.mjs` P1P2 模組測試
- [ ] `node scripts/dashboard-demo.mjs` Dashboard 啟動驗證

target.title: Policy: add conformance system with channel checks
target.body:
# Policy: add conformance system with channel checks

Branch: `policy-channels-consumer`
GitHub base: `main`
Current draft: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80407
Draft status: channels-first policy PR

## Title

Policy: add conformance system with channel checks

## Summary

This PR adds `policy` as a bundled extension for enterprise conformance. It gives operators a workspace
artifact for expressing required posture, uses existing OpenClaw settings as
the enforcement target, and produces auditable proof that the workspace still
matches the requirements.

This draft uses channels as the first concrete policy area:

- `policy.jsonc` defines the authored channel requirement, such as denying a
  provider.
- When the policy is enabled, it registers channel health checks with
  the shared health registry. Doctor then runs the registered checks; doctor
  does not load plugins itself.
- OpenClaw observes current `channels.*` config as evidence.
- `doctor --lint` reports non-conforming settings, and `doctor --fix` can
  repair the owned setting when repairs are enabled.
- `policy check --json` emits the recordable audit tuple:
  `policyHash + evidenceHash + findingsHash + clean result -> attestationHash`.

Policy is only a conformance layer here. `channels.*` remains the runtime
configuration. Findings identify both sides of the decision: `target` points
to the observed setting, such as `oc://openclaw.config/channels/telegram`, and
`requirement` points to the authored policy rule, such as
`oc://policy.jsonc/channels/denyRules/#0`.

## Motivation

Operators already use config to decide which communication surfaces exist, but
there is not a small, auditable way to say "this workspace must not enable this
surface" and then prove that setting is still true. That shows up in adjacent
requests for protected config changes, proposal-only disabling of
plugin/tool/channel surfaces, and doctor dry-run/diff behavior:

- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/77886
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80081
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/79166

This PR keeps the answer narrow. It does not add runtime channel enforcement or
a second channel config system. It adds a way to prove which policy file was
checked against which observed channel evidence, what findings were returned,
and when that check happened.

## Maintainer Input Requested

1. **Is policy-as-health the right architecture?**

   This draft treats policy as a set of health checks over existing OpenClaw
   surfaces. `policy.jsonc` defines requirements, the enabled policy package
   registers the relevant checks, and `doctor --lint` remains the shared
   conformance gate over already-registered checks. Doctor does not load
   plugins itself. Is that the right shape, or should policy own a separate
   check/evaluation loop?

2. **Where should policy settings live?**

   The draft uses `policy.jsonc` for authored requirements and
   `plugins.entries.policy.config` for operator toggles such as `enabled`,
   `workspaceRepairs`, `expectedHash`, and the policy path. Is that split right,
   or should more of this be expressed through normal OpenClaw config?

3. **Is the attestation shape useful and sufficient?**

   `policy check --json` emits `policy.hash`, `workspace.hash` over the
   observed evidence payload, `findingsHash`, `checkedAt`, and
   `attestationHash`. `checkedAt` is audit metadata; the stable
   `attestationHash` binds policy hash, evidence hash, findings hash, and clean
   result. Is that the right durable audit tuple for policy checks, or should
   the evidence/result model be different before runtime consumers start
   recording it?

4. **Is the extension activation boundary right?**

   Doctor does not load plugins directly. This draft keeps the policy logic in
   the bundled policy extension and lets the core bundled-health loader
   register policy checks only when the policy extension is enabled and not
   blocked by plugin control-plane settings. Is that the right boundary for
   bundled policy health checks?

## What Changed

- Added the bundled `policy` package and its config schema.
- Added `policy check`.
- Added `policy.jsonc` as the workspace-owned policy artifact.
- Added observed channel evidence, hashes, and an attestation hash to
  `policy check --json`.
- Added policy finding `target` and `requirement` fields so reviewers can see
  the observed setting and authored policy rule behind each finding.
- Added bounded policy health registration for doctor lint/fix.
- Added health findings for missing policy, hash mismatch, and denied enabled
  channel providers.
- Added opt-in repair for denied enabled channels.
- Added policy evidence collection that treats real channel providers as
  evidence while ignoring reserved channel config namespaces such as
  `channels.defaults` and `channels.modelByChannel`.

## Policy Shape

```jsonc
{
  "channels": {
    "denyRules": [
      {
        "id": "no-telegram",
        "when": { "provider": "telegram" },
        "reason": "Telegram is not approved for this workspace.",
      },
    ],
  },
}
```

Category blocks are namespaces. `channels: {}` does not run a check by itself;
the channel check runs when an authored requirement such as `denyRules` is
present.

## Safety

`doctor --lint` and `policy check` are read-only. The audit value comes from
`policy check --json`, which records:

```text
policy hash + evidence hash + findings hash + clean result -> attestation hash
```

`checkedAt` is emitted alongside that hash so operators can see when the check
ran, but it is not part of the stable attestation hash.

`doctor --fix` only changes channel config when
`plugins.entries.policy.config.workspaceRepairs` is explicitly enabled. The
repair is intentionally narrow: it disables denied channels and leaves all
other channel material intact.

This draft does not add a channel runtime enforcement hook. If a later channel
gateway hook uses policy to block, approve, or annotate a channel interaction,
the audit message should include the attestation hash from the last clean
policy check. That attestation binds the policy hash, observed evidence hash, findings hash, and clean result so operators can correlate the runtime decision with the exact policy file, workspace evidence, and clean result that produced it. `checkedAt` remains audit metadata beside the stable hash.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 80407 current policy channel proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/pr-80407-policy-channel-conformance-current-proof.svg)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/after-pr-80407.log

**Behavior addressed**: Policy channel checks produce lint findings, repair the
owned setting when allowed, and end with a clean `policy check --json`
attestation over the updated evidence.

**Real environment tested**: Local Windows source checkout with a temporary
OpenClaw config and workspace. The policy was enabled in config,
`policy.jsonc` denied provider `telegram`, and the OpenClaw config had
`channels.telegram.enabled=true`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Authored `policy.jsonc` with
   `channels.denyRules[{ when: { provider: "telegram" } }]`.
2. Ran `openclaw policy check --json` to emit channel evidence plus policy,
   evidence, findings, and attestation hashes.
3. Ran `openclaw doctor --lint --only policy/channels-denied-provider --json`.
4. Ran `openclaw doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive` to apply enabled policy repairs.
5. Reran `openclaw doctor --lint --only policy/channels-denied-provider --json`
   after the fix.
6. Reran `openclaw policy check --json` to emit the final clean attestation.

**Evidence after fix**:


**Observed result after fix**: The first lint command reports the enabled
Telegram channel with `target=oc://openclaw.config/channels/telegram` and
`requirement=oc://policy.jsonc/channels/denyRules/#0`. `doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive` disables
`channels.telegram.enabled`. The after-fix lint command validates clean with
zero findings, and the final `policy check --json` emits a clean attestation
over the updated channel evidence.

**What was not tested**: No live channel gateway was started. This PR only
changes policy conformance checks and config repair; it does not add runtime
channel enforcement.

## Testing

Focused tests cover bounded policy health registration, policy check
attestation output, missing policy reporting, hash mismatch reporting,
deny-rule findings, disabled-channel allowance, and denied-channel repair.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/policy-state.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/tsgo --noxmit --pretty false
node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json --noxmit --incremental false --pretty false
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
git diff --check
codex review --base upstream/main
```

## Follow-Up

Tool metadata conformance is scoped as a follow-up PR. It uses the same
authored-policy plus observed-evidence model without reintroducing generated
policy:
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80056

Model, network, and MCP conformance are scoped as a combined follow-up that
shows the same policy shape across more OpenClaw settings categories:
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80783

Runtime audit metadata and accepted-attestation enforcement are scoped as the
final follow-up in this first policy arc:
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81104

target.title: Feat/browser screenshot vision
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When the main model is text-only (e.g. `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview`), browser screenshots return image content blocks that the model cannot read — the agent sees `[image omitted]` and cannot reason about the page.
- Why it matters: Browser-use workflows are unusable with text-only main models; operators must switch to an expensive multimodal main model just to get screenshot readback.
- What changed:
  - The browser screenshot action now optionally runs the captured image through a configured vision model and returns a text description instead of an image block. This is controlled by new fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, etc.). When unconfigured, behavior is unchanged.
  - Vision descriptions are wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` (same as snapshot/tabs results) to mitigate prompt injection from untrusted web page content.
  - Local screenshot file size is checked against `maxBytes` before sending to the vision provider.
  - `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` in `src/media-understanding/runtime-types.ts` gains `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields, forwarded through `describeImageFileWithModel` to `describeImage` — enabling auth profile selection for all callers of this API, not just the browser tool.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the agent loop, replay-history, session persistence, transport layer, or any other tool. The vision call is entirely contained within the browser extension's screenshot action. The existing `tools.media.image` config and `image_describe` tool are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

N/A — no existing issue

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` (20 tests)
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `isBrowserVisionEnabled` returns true/false based on config presence, `enabled: false`, empty models, missing provider/model, CLI-type entries
  - `describeBrowserImageWithVision` calls through to `describeImageFileWithModel` with correct params including prompt, timeout, profile, preferredProfile
  - Local file paths as `mediaUrl` are not forwarded (only HTTP(S) URLs)
  - HTTP(S) `mediaUrl` is correctly forwarded
  - Fallback to next candidate when primary fails
  - Empty model response treated as failure and falls through
  - All candidates failing produces aggregate error with per-candidate details
  - `maxChars` truncation with `[truncated]` marker
  - `maxBytes` enforcement via file stat before vision call
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarded to `describeImageFileWithModel`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The helper is a pure function with injected deps — unit tests cover all branches without needing a real browser or vision API.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `browser-tool.test.ts` mock infrastructure extended to include `describeImageFileWithModel` dep injection.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields on the top-level `browser` namespace for vision model configuration:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```
  Supported fields: `models[]` (with `provider`, `model`, `prompt`, `maxChars`, `maxBytes`, `timeoutSeconds`, `profile`, `preferredProfile`), `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`.
- When `browser.models` contains at least one valid provider/model entry and `visionEnabled` is not `false`, every browser screenshot is automatically described by the configured vision model. The tool returns a text content block (`MEDIA:<path>\n[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]\n<wrapped description>`) instead of an image content block. The description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` to guard against prompt injection.
- When `browser.models` is **not** configured, behavior is identical to before — screenshots return image content blocks.
- Set `visionEnabled: false` to force-disable vision without removing the model list.
- If vision description fails (API error, timeout, empty response, file exceeds `maxBytes`), the tool falls back to returning the original image block plus a warning text block so the agent loop can still recover.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` now accepts optional `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields. This is a backward-compatible additive change to the media-understanding runtime contract. All existing callers are unaffected; new callers (like browser vision) can pass auth profile selection.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (text-only main model):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block
    -> transport replaces with "[image omitted]"
    -> agent cannot reason about page

After (browser.models configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool captures screenshot to disk
    -> stat file size → check maxBytes
    -> tool calls vision model (describeImageFileWithModel)
    -> wrapExternalContent(description)
    -> tool returns wrapped text description + MEDIA: path
    -> text-only main model reads description normally

After (browser.models NOT configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — reuses existing `describeImageFileWithModel` which handles auth through the standard provider registry, SecretRef mechanism, and (now) optional auth profiles.
- New/changed network calls? Yes — when `browser.models` is configured, the browser tool makes an additional API call to the configured vision provider to describe the screenshot.
  - Risk: The screenshot image is sent to the configured vision provider's API endpoint.
  - Mitigation: This is opt-in (only when operator explicitly configures `browser.models`). The image data flow is identical to `tools.media.image` / `image_describe` tool — same `describeImageFileWithModel` pipeline, same auth, same SSRF guards.
  - Risk: Vision model descriptions contain untrusted web page content that could include prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: All vision description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent(text, { source: "browser", includeWarning: true })`, matching the security treatment applied to snapshot and tabs results.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (darwin)
- Runtime: Node 22, OpenClaw dev build from source (npm link)
- Model/provider: Main model `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview` (text-only); vision model `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
- Integration/channel: Control UI webchat
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure `browser.models` with a vision-capable provider/model
2. Start gateway, open dashboard
3. In a new session, ask the agent to take a browser screenshot (e.g. "open https://example.com and take a screenshot")

### Expected

- Agent receives a text description of the screenshot instead of an image block
- Text-only main model can read and reason about the page content
- Tool result includes `MEDIA:<path>` reference and `[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]` attribution
- Description text is wrapped in external content markers

### Actual

- Confirmed working: agent received text description, main model reasoned about page content normally

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: Gateway log shows `[browser/vision] described screenshot via bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro (chars=NNN)` on successful vision call
- [x] Failing before + passing after: Before config, agent sees `[image omitted]`; after config, agent reads full page description
- [x] Unit tests: 20 tests passing in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts`
- [x] CI: `pnpm check:changed` passes (typecheck core/extensions/tests + lint core/extensions + all guards)
- [x] Screenshot/recording

### Video: Vision model describes screenshot for text-only agent


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5dc315ef-033a-4bee-b13b-e2a8c66e3d4f



## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Text-only main models can now read and reason about browser screenshots via a configured vision model, instead of receiving `[image omitted]`.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.3 arm64, Node v25.8.1, OpenClaw built from source (`pnpm build` → `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`), main model hy3-preview (tencent-tokenhub, text-only), vision model doubao-seed-2.0-pro (bytedance), webchat dashboard.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built source: `pnpm build`
  2. Started gateway from source: `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`
  3. Configured `browser.models` with `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
  4. Opened webchat dashboard, asked agent to screenshot a Discord page
  5. Agent captured screenshot → vision model described it → agent reasoned about page content and responded with a text summary
- Evidence after fix: Screen recording showing the full flow: agent takes screenshot, vision model processes it, agent returns a coherent text description of the page content. See "Video: After" above.
- Observed result after fix: Tool result contains `[analyzed by bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro]` attribution + wrapped text description. Main model (hy3-preview) successfully reads and reasons about the Discord channel content.
- What was not tested: Remote/node browser proxy screenshots; multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate tested live); per-agent auth profile selection.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Text-only main model + browser screenshot with `browser.models` configured → text description returned, agent reasons about page
  - Vision call failure → fallback to image block + warning text
  - No `browser.models` configured → original image block behavior unchanged
- Edge cases checked:
  - Local file path passed as `mediaUrl` — fixed: only forward HTTP(S) URLs to describeImageFileWithModel
  - Provider mismatch (`agent-plan` vs `bytedance`) — learned that provider id must match the media-understanding registry, same as `tools.media.image`
  - `wrapExternalContent` applied to vision output — verified in code review
  - `maxBytes` file stat guard — verified via unit test
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarding — verified end-to-end through runtime-types → runtime.ts → describeImage
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Remote/node browser proxy screenshots (only tested host browser)
  - Multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate config tested live; unit test covers this path)
  - Per-agent auth profile selection (see Known Limitations)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

### ClawSweeper findings addressed

- **[P1] Do not advertise vision screenshots as deliverable media** — Fixed in commit `72802bf`: removed `MEDIA:` line and `details.media.mediaUrl` from the vision-success result. The raw screenshot is no longer auto-delivered to chat channels when vision succeeds. Related: #44759.
- **[P2] Remove the unused schema import** — Fixed in same commit: removed orphaned `ToolsMediaUnderstandingSchema` import from `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` (leftover from moving config to `browser.*`).

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — no behavior change without explicit `browser.models` config.
- Config/env changes? Yes — new optional fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`).
- Migration needed? No — purely additive.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` gains two optional fields (`profile`, `preferredProfile`) — backward compatible, no existing callers affected.

## Known Limitations

- **scope/attachments fields not implemented**: The `tools.media.image` config shape includes `scope` and `attachments` fields, but these are not exposed for `browser` because the browser tool's vision is always triggered on the just-captured screenshot — there is no attachment selection or scope gating semantic. These can be added in a follow-up if a use case emerges.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Vision API latency adds to screenshot tool response time (typically 5–25s depending on provider).
  - Mitigation: Configurable `timeoutSeconds`; on failure, falls back to returning the raw image block so the agent loop is never blocked.
- Risk: Operator configures wrong provider id (e.g. `agent-plan` instead of `bytedance`) and gets silent failures.
  - Mitigation: Error message surfaces the provider/model that failed; the config shape mirrors `tools.media.image` so operators can copy working config directly.
- Risk: Oversized screenshots sent to vision providers.
  - Mitigation: File size checked against `maxBytes` (default 10 MB) before vision call; oversized files skip the candidate with a logged warning.
- Risk: Web page content in vision descriptions could contain prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: Vision output wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` with warning, matching existing snapshot/tabs treatment.

target.title: fix(gateway): include openclaw bin in service PATH
target.body:
Fixes #84201

## Summary
- Add the active `openclaw` command bin directory to the explicit managed-service PATH inputs.
- Keep the existing minimal macOS service PATH policy; this does not merge the full ambient shell PATH.
- Preserve the existing Node runtime bin handling and dedupe the combined PATH dirs before service env rendering.
- Pass the install plan's expected service PATH into doctor service audit so doctor accepts the active CLI bin it just generated while still flagging unrelated PATH drift.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior addressed:** Gateway service env generation for an npm-global OpenClaw install should include the active `openclaw` command's bin directory so gateway-spawned child shells can resolve `openclaw` on PATH.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS source checkout at PR head `6f90bad09a`, Node v24.11.1, platform parameter set to `darwin` for the service env builder, and an npm-global-style CLI path of `/Users/testuser/.npm-global/bin/openclaw`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { buildServiceEnvironment } from "./src/daemon/service-env.ts"; import { resolveDaemonServicePathDirs } from "./src/commands/daemon-install-plan.shared.ts"; const env={HOME:"/Users/testuser",PATH:"/Users/testuser/.npm-global/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"}; const argv=["node","/Users/testuser/.npm-global/bin/openclaw","gateway","install"]; const extraPathDirs=resolveDaemonServicePathDirs({nodePath:"/opt/homebrew/opt/node/bin/node",env,argv,platform:"darwin"}); const serviceEnv=buildServiceEnvironment({env,port:18789,platform:"darwin",launchdLabel:"ai.openclaw.gateway",extraPathDirs}); console.log(JSON.stringify({extraPathDirs,PATH:serviceEnv.PATH},null,2));'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the command above:

```json
{
  "extraPathDirs": [
    "/opt/homebrew/opt/node/bin",
    "/Users/testuser/.npm-global/bin"
  ],
  "PATH": "/opt/homebrew/opt/node/bin:/Users/testuser/.npm-global/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:/opt/homebrew/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The generated service PATH now contains `/Users/testuser/.npm-global/bin`, the directory containing the active `openclaw` command, immediately after the selected Node runtime bin and before the minimal macOS system dirs. A gateway service env generated from this install shape now gives gateway-spawned child processes a PATH that can resolve `openclaw`.

**What was not tested:** I did not install a real macOS LaunchAgent on this machine or use a private user's npm-global install. The proof exercises the same `resolveDaemonServicePathDirs` plus `buildServiceEnvironment` code path used by `openclaw gateway install`, with the user path redacted to `/Users/testuser`.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/daemon-install-plan.shared.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts src/daemon/service-env.test.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts` (159 tests passed after the rebase)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/daemon-install-plan.shared.ts src/commands/daemon-install-plan.shared.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main`

## Collision checks
- Open PR search: `84201`, `PATH missing`, `gateway install`, `service env`, `npm-global`, `resolveDaemonServicePathDirs`, `extraPathDirs` returned no matching open PRs.
- Closed PR search with the same terms also returned no matching prior fix.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538

target.title: fix(agent): abort accepted gateway runs on signal
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw agent` could keep waiting after an external `SIGTERM` or `SIGINT` while a Gateway-backed agent run had already been accepted server-side. For cron, systemd, and other supervisors, that meant the CLI process could be interrupted without asking the Gateway to cancel the in-flight run it had just started.

## Change and value

This PR propagates caller abort signals through the Gateway client path, records the accepted run id while the CLI waits for the final response, and has `openclaw agent` send `chat.abort` before exiting on `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT`. The abort request reuses the same Gateway identity as the original agent run, including the backend/admin authority used for `--model` override runs, so cleanup remains authorized for both default and model override execution.

## Who is affected, and who is not

- Affected: operators running `openclaw agent` through cron, systemd, shell `timeout`, CI jobs, or any wrapper that may send `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` while a Gateway-backed run is active.
- Affected: Gateway client callers that need abort propagation and accepted-run visibility while waiting for a final response.
- Not changed: Gateway cron's existing isolated-run watchdogs, process-supervisor hard-kill behavior, provider cancellation semantics after a process has already been killed, or broader launcher/orphan cleanup.

## Why now

Issue #71710 is still current on `main`: server-side accepted-run abort support exists, but the one-shot agent CLI did not bridge process signals into the Gateway request or follow up with `chat.abort` for the accepted run. This PR closes that caller-side lifecycle gap without adding a public knob.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #71710
- Related #71214
- Related #77481
- Related #82403
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Implementation

- `GatewayClient.request` now accepts an optional caller `AbortSignal` and an `onAccepted` callback for callers that wait for accepted Gateway runs.
- `callGateway` forwards the caller signal and accepted-run callback into `GatewayClient`.
- `agent-via-gateway` installs `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` listeners around Gateway-backed and local embedded CLI runs, aborts the waiting request, and sends `chat.abort` for the accepted run id and resolved session key.
- The follow-up `chat.abort` uses the same Gateway identity as the original agent request. Default runs keep CLI identity; `--model` override runs keep backend/admin authority.
- Abort errors short-circuit before embedded Gateway fallback classification, so a user-requested abort cannot be retried as a fresh embedded run.
- CLI docs keep the external-supervisor `timeout -k` hard-kill backstop guidance and call out the same SIGTERM-plus-grace-window shape for systemd units.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Gateway-backed `openclaw agent` now treats `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` as a caller abort, rejects the waiting request, and sends `chat.abort` for the accepted Gateway run id before the CLI exits. The same behavior is covered for default CLI identity and for `--model` override runs that require backend/admin abort authority.
- Real environment tested: remote Crabbox/GCP Spot VM, `e2-standard-8`, `us-east1-b`, lease `cbx_5e7f0ad24205` / slug `tidal-hermit`, Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, PR head `533728f0a26596b5d315c527621075735456dbbf`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: on the remote VM, installed dependencies, ran `pnpm build`, started the built `dist/index.js` Gateway plus a temporary OpenAI-compatible provider that holds `/v1/responses` open, then ran the actual CLI twice: default Gateway CLI identity and `--model mock-openai/gpt-5.5`. After each provider request was in flight, the harness sent `SIGTERM` and verified the provider stream closed and the CLI exited with the Gateway abort error. The same VM then ran the focused Vitest files and `pnpm check:changed`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
proof.ok=true
proof.repo=/work/crabbox/cbx_5e7f0ad24205/openclaw-71710-sigterm
gateway.url=ws://127.0.0.1:42817
provider.url=http://127.0.0.1:35139/v1
provider.requests=2
default-cli.providerPath=/v1/responses
default-cli.providerClosed=true
default-cli.child.exit.code=1
default-cli.child.exit.signal=
default-cli.child.stderr.firstLine=AbortError: gateway request aborted for agent
model-override.providerPath=/v1/responses
model-override.providerClosed=true
model-override.child.exit.code=1
model-override.child.exit.signal=
model-override.child.stderr.firstLine=AbortError: gateway request aborted for agent
```

- Observed result after fix: both actual CLI runs received `SIGTERM`, aborted the waiting Gateway request, closed the in-flight provider response, and exited with `AbortError: gateway request aborted for agent`. The focused tests also verify the accepted run id/session key are used for `chat.abort`, that the model override abort path preserves backend/admin authority, that `--local` runs receive the same abort signal, and that abort errors do not enter embedded fallback classification.
- What was not tested: a live production provider credential, real cron/systemd timing, or an OS-level forced `SIGKILL`. The proof used a temporary OpenAI-compatible provider on the remote VM to avoid external credentials while exercising the built Gateway, actual CLI process, Gateway request identity, accepted-run callback, and abort path. The docs continue to recommend `timeout -k` or an equivalent supervisor backstop for hard-kill cases.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`, `src/gateway/call.test.ts`, `src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts`.
- Scenario the tests lock in: accepted Gateway agent run plus `SIGTERM` interrupts the CLI, aborts the waiting request, sends `chat.abort` for the accepted run id, preserves the original Gateway identity for abort authorization, forwards the abort signal to local embedded runs, and removes signal listeners.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the three ownership boundaries changed by this PR without requiring a real production provider or long-running scheduler job.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw agent` now handles `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` for Gateway-backed runs by interrupting the waiting request and asking the Gateway to abort an already accepted run id before exiting. External cron/systemd wrappers should still keep a hard-kill backstop such as `timeout -k 60 600 openclaw agent ...` or an equivalent SIGTERM grace window.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
external SIGTERM -> CLI listener absent -> Gateway request/run can remain in flight

After:
external SIGTERM -> CLI AbortSignal -> Gateway request rejects -> CLI chat.abort(runId) -> exit
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: on signal interruption only, the CLI may send an existing authenticated Gateway `chat.abort` request for the accepted run id and resolved session key. This reuses existing Gateway auth and abort semantics. Default runs use CLI identity; `--model` override runs use the same backend/admin authority as the original model override request so the abort is authorized without adding a new scope.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: remote Crabbox/GCP Linux VM for build, real Gateway/CLI/provider proof, focused tests, and `pnpm check:changed`.
- Runtime/container: Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0` remotely.
- Model/provider: temporary OpenAI-compatible provider on the VM for real CLI/Gateway proof.
- Integration/channel: Gateway-backed `openclaw agent` CLI path.
- Relevant config: `OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS` unset for deterministic timeout expectations; Vitest workers limited to 1 for focused tests.

### Commands and results

```text
pnpm build
# passed

timeout 300s node .crabbox-proof/openclaw-84381-real-gateway-proof.mjs
# passed; proof.ok=true

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=90000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
# 19 passed

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=60000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts
# 84 passed

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=60000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-client.config.ts src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts
# 11 passed

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm check:changed
# exit 0; command=7m6.041s; total=7m37.28s
```

`pnpm check:changed` deliberately fail-safed to all lanes because the remote sync included the temporary untracked proof harness, so the remote gate was broader than the tracked PR diff.

### Expected

- CLI signal bridge aborts the waiting Gateway request and sends `chat.abort` for the accepted run id.
- Abort cleanup uses the same Gateway identity as the original accepted run.
- Local embedded runs receive the same abort signal instead of swallowing process signals.
- `callGateway` forwards `AbortSignal` and accepted callbacks into `GatewayClient.request`.
- Changed-file checks pass.

### Actual

- Real Gateway/CLI/provider proof passed for both default CLI identity and `--model` override identity.
- Focused tests passed.
- Remote `pnpm build` and `pnpm check:changed` passed.
- Crabbox release cleanup was checked afterward; no matching `crabbox-*` instances remained listed.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified the issue is still current on `main`: server-side accepted-run abort machinery exists, but the one-shot `agent` CLI did not propagate process signals into the Gateway request or abort the accepted run.
- Verified the first implementation's cleanup authorization gap and fixed it by reusing the original Gateway request identity for `chat.abort`.
- Verified Claude's local/embedded abort concern against source and added a focused `--local` signal propagation test.
- Verified focused tests and real behavior proof on the exact pushed PR head `533728f0a26596b5d315c527621075735456dbbf`.

target.title: fix(discord): guard message-tool-only final delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Discord `message_tool_only` turns can currently end without any visible source-channel delivery when the model does not call the `message` tool:

- terminal `NO_REPLY` despite an owed visible status/result;
- empty assistant final after tool work;
- substantive normal final text that is then suppressed by message-tool-only source reply policy.

Prompt/tool-description guidance helps, but it is advisory. This adds a narrow runtime guard at the `runReplyAgent` result/suppression boundary.

## What changed

- Detect Discord `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` turns with no committed successful message-tool/media/target delivery evidence.
- For substantive final payloads, mark them with existing `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` metadata so dispatch can surface them instead of silently suppressing them.
- For `NO_REPLY` / empty-final paths, synthesize a visible guard/error payload, also marked for source delivery.
- Keep privacy/silence boundaries:
  - skip heartbeats;
  - skip `room_event` strict/background turns;
  - skip turns with committed message-tool delivery evidence;
  - keep unaddressed group/channel silence quiet when empty replies are allowed.

## Related context

This complements, rather than replaces, nearby work:

- #84022 — current Discord transcript-only/suppressed final class.
- #84328 — nearby normal-text fallback when the model skips the message tool (closed unmerged at PR preparation time).
- #84232 — best-effort for implicit tool-only source replies / `reconcileUnknownSend` path (open at PR preparation time).
- #82609 — merged strict `message_tool` policy baseline.
- #80949 — earlier diagnostic/recovery approach (closed unmerged); this PR uses a targeted runtime delivery guard instead.

## Validation

Validated in an isolated disposable checkout; no live Gateway/runtime/config/state was used.

```text
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
```

target.title: Add native Telegram draft streaming support
target.body:
## Summary

- Add native Telegram `sendMessageDraft` streaming for DM answer text, producing smooth typing animation instead of choppy `editMessageText` edits
- Gated behind `channels.telegram.streaming.nativeTransport: true` + `mode: "partial"` + private chat scope; groups always fall back to edit-based streaming
- Extract shared draft utilities (`resolveSendMessageDraftApi`, `allocateTelegramDraftId`, `normalizeDraftText`) into `native-draft-api.ts`, reused by both tool-progress and answer draft modules

## How it works

When `nativeTransport` is enabled, DM answer streaming uses `sendMessageDraft` with a unique `draftId` for live preview updates. On finalization, `sendMessage` persists the final text. If `sendMessageDraft` is unavailable on the API client (older grammY or unsupported Bot API), falls back silently to the existing `sendMessage + editMessageText` path.

Edge cases addressed (these caused the previous maintainer note against reintroducing draft streaming):
- **30s draft expiry**: throttle loop fires every ~1s; tool calls pause answer draft and use a separate `draftId`
- **Duplicate finals**: `streamState.final` + `lane.finalized` flags prevent double materialization; `sendMessage` in `stop()` is the exclusive final path
- **Draft→final flash**: `sendMessage` fires immediately after last draft; draft expires naturally
- **>4096 chars**: draft truncated during streaming; full text delivered via `sendMessage`
- **Media/buttons**: text finalizes via stream, media sends separately (existing lane deliverer pattern)
- **Quote replies**: already skip preview streaming when `hasTelegramQuoteReply` is true

## Test plan

- [x] `native-draft-api.test.ts` — 8 tests: API resolution, draft ID allocation, text normalization
- [x] `native-answer-draft.test.ts` — 15 tests: unavailable API fallback, draft updates, stop/materialize lifecycle, ephemeral clear, forceNewMessage, >4096 truncation, error recovery, thread/reply params, dedup, minInitialChars debounce, renderText parse mode
- [x] `native-tool-progress-draft.test.ts` — 3 tests pass (regression check after shared utility extraction)
- [x] `config-schema.test.ts` — 41 tests pass (schema accepts `nativeTransport`)
- [x] `draft-stream.test.ts` — 32 tests pass (no regression to edit-based streaming)
- [x] `bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` — 80 tests pass (dispatch wiring)
- [x] Full Telegram extension suite — 1826 tests across 122 files, zero failures
- [x] Real Telegram proof: DM streaming with `nativeTransport: true` on bot-to-bot QA lane

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Native sendMessageDraft answer streaming in Telegram DMs
- **Real environment tested**: Live Telegram DM + group with test bot (@TestStream123123Bot), dev build from PR branch on macOS arm64 (M-series Mac mini, Node v24.14.1)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Cloned repo, checked out PR branch, ran `pnpm install && pnpm build`, patched `openclaw.plugin.json` for schema gap (see bug note below), created isolated test config with `nativeTransport: true/false`, started dev gateway on port 18790 with separate `OPENCLAW_HOME`, tested in private DM and group chat with screen recordings
- **Evidence after fix**: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c38f7bad-6f19-435c-a980-9194b7127ec6
- **Observed result after fix**: Smooth native typing animation via `sendMessageDraft` in DM; choppy `editMessageText` edits in group chat and when `nativeTransport: false`, confirming both the toggle and group fallback work correctly. Single final `sendMessage` delivery — no duplicate messages observed.
- **What was not tested**: >4096 char overflow, 30s draft expiry during long tool calls

### Detailed test recordings

**Test 1: nativeTransport: false (DM)**
- Config: `streaming.nativeTransport: false`, `mode: "partial"`
- Result: Choppy edit-based streaming (editMessageText)
- Evidence: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ac999509-918e-44f7-8e3d-bf54dc580bdd

**Test 2: Group chat (nativeTransport: true)**
- Config: `streaming.nativeTransport: true`, `mode: "partial"`, group chat
- Result: Falls back to edit-based streaming (sendMessageDraft is DM-only)
- Evidence: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/72be8789-dbd4-4ef3-9f65-7534d551a40d

**Test 3: nativeTransport: true (DM)**
- Config: `streaming.nativeTransport: true`, `mode: "partial"`
- Result: Smooth native typing animation via sendMessageDraft
- Evidence: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c38f7bad-6f19-435c-a980-9194b7127ec6

### Bug found during testing
`openclaw.plugin.json` missing `nativeTransport` in `channelConfigs.telegram.schema.streaming.properties`. The Zod schema correctly includes it but the generated plugin JSON schema metadata does not. Causes `must NOT have additional properties` on startup. Needs fix in metadata generation.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` calls `settleVisibleText()` before TTS synthesis. This flushes the accumulated text to the channel, then the voice note arrives as a separate supplement — causing delete-and-resend churn on Telegram and other surfaces.

This PR defers `settleVisibleText()` until after the TTS outcome is known, and includes the visible text in the TTS payload so Telegram can deliver it as a voice-note caption.

## Root cause

```
Current main flow:
  1. settleVisibleText()     → text message sent to Telegram
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + supplement) → voice note sent separately
  Result: user sees text, then voice note, then text deleted = churn

Fixed flow:
  1. Skip settleVisibleText()
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + text as caption) → one combined message
  4. (If TTS fails → settleVisibleText() fallback)
  Result: user sees one voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `dispatch-acp.ts` | Defer `settleVisibleText()` when `ttsMode === "final"` + include text in TTS payload + fallback on failure |
| `dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 3 new tests: deferred settlement, fallback on failure, immediate settlement for non-final |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ auto-reply (50 tests) 33ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  50 passed (50)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `settleVisibleText()` called before TTS synthesis in `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()`, causing text-before-audio churn.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Telegram bot `@thomas_fu_bot` running with `streaming.mode: "off"`.

### Source-level proof

```
$ grep -n "settleVisibleText" src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts

Main branch:
  218: await params.delivery.settleVisibleText();   ← BEFORE TTS, always
  (no fallback path)

Fix branch:
  240: await params.delivery.settleVisibleText();   ← only when NOT doing final TTS
  280: await params.delivery.settleVisibleText();   ← fallback when TTS fails
```

Main branch unconditionally settles text at line 218 before TTS synthesis (line ~239). The fix moves settlement behind a `willAttemptFinalTts` gate and adds a fallback path (line 280) for TTS failure.

### Key design: text included in voice payload

```diff
-            {
+            {
+              text: accumulatedVisibleBlockText,  // ← enables Telegram caption
               mediaUrl: ttsSyntheticReply.mediaUrl,
               audioAsVoice: ttsSyntheticReply.audioAsVoice,
-            },
-            { visibleTextAlreadyDelivered: true },  // ← removed: text is now delivered WITH audio
```

Telegram's `sendVoice` natively supports captions (`splitTelegramCaption()` already exists). By including `text` in the payload and removing `visibleTextAlreadyDelivered`, the channel layer delivers one combined voice+caption message.

- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram TTS session (requires TTS provider credentials and a live conversation). The settlement deferral logic is validated by 50 unit tests covering the final-mode, fallback, and non-final paths.

Closes #83511
