target.title: feat(observability): add configurable session tracing headers for LLM requests
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:


- Problem: Users need to correlate individual runs and group related requests/subagents in external dashboards (e.g., Helicone.ai, DodoRouter.com).
- Why it matters: Easier tracing on at a "run" level not single log requests.
- What changed: Adds opt-in session tracing headers (x-session-id, x-session-name) to all outgoing LLM provider requests, enabling cost tracking and request grouping in observability platforms.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause:
- Missing detection / guardrail:
- Contributing context (if known):

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — new feature with full test coverage in observability-session-headers.test.ts

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
- Scenario the test should lock in:
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
- If no new test is added, why not:

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

New config option under diagnostics.sessionTracing:

```
{
  "diagnostics": {
    "sessionTracing": {
      "enabled": true,
      "headers": {
        "sessionId": "x-session-id",
        "sessionName": "x-session-name"
      },
      "sessionName": "OpenClaw"
    }
  }
}
```

When enabled, every provider request includes:
- x-session-id: <runId> — unique per request/run
- x-session-name: <sessionKey> — groups related requests/subagents

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user action] -> [old state]

After:
[user action] -> [new state] -> [result]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No — only adds headers to existing calls
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS:
- Runtime/container:
- Model/provider:
- Integration/channel (if any):
- Relevant config (redacted):

### Steps

1. Add diagnostics.sessionTracing.enabled: true to config
2. Optionally customize header names for your observability provider
3. Send a message
4. Verify headers in provider request logs/dashboard (can sign up on DodoRouter.com to confirm they're coming through).

### Expected

-

### Actual

-

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
Added tracing with `x-session-id` and `x-session-name` params and sent to DodoRouter.com. Grouping is working.
- Edge cases checked:
- What you did **not** verify: Traced the code and seems like the case but I did not verify if messages from channels (e.g. Telegram) get a new runid each time (which is desired to make it easier to debug).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

 Backward compatible? Yes — opt-in via config, no headers sent unless enabled
- Config/env changes? Yes — new optional diagnostics.sessionTracing block
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Headers may conflict with caller-provided headers
  - Mitigation: Caller headers take precedence (spread order in wrapStreamFnWithSessionTracing)
- Risk: Sensitive session keys in headers
  - Mitigation: Only sent when explicitly enabled; falls back to sessionKey which is already known to the system

target.title: fix: block empty exec commands + consecutive tool-error circuit breaker (plus minor UI test stability)
target.body:
**Summary**
- Add a guard that blocks `exec`/`bash` tool calls when the `command` parameter is missing or blank, preventing empty-command execution loops.
- Add a consecutive tool-error counter and a circuit-breaker callback in the embedded subscribe tool handler chain; trip after 3 consecutive tool errors.
- Stabilize UI `formatNextRun()` weekday output across locales so tests don’t fail on non-English environments.

**Motivation / Context**
- Following `exec-loop-fix.md`, we observed cases where empty `exec` commands could lead to ineffective retry/loop behavior.
- We implement two layers of protection:
  1) pre-call validation to reject empty command invocations,
  2) runtime circuit breaker to stop repeated tool failures and surface a deterministic failure signal.
- Additionally, UI tests were flaky on systems whose default locale is not English (weekday formatting output differed).

**Implementation Details**
- **Layer 1 (pre-tool-call guard)**  
  - Normalize tool name (`exec`/`bash`) and validate params.
  - If `command` is missing/empty/whitespace-only, return `{ blocked: true, reason }` and log a warning, preventing the call from running.
- **Layer 2 (consecutive-error circuit breaker)**  
  - Introduce state tracking for consecutive tool errors and an `onConsecutiveToolError` callback.
  - Increment on tool failures and trip after 3 consecutive errors; invoke the callback to stop/redirect the flow.
- **UI test stability**  
  - `formatNextRun()` now formats the weekday with a fixed locale (`en-US`) to make output deterministic across environments.

**Key Files Changed**
- `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.ts` (block empty exec/bash command)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.types.ts` (error-state types)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.types.ts` (callback type)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts` (state initialization)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` (counter + threshold logic)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` (runner integration)
- `ui/src/ui/presenter.ts` (locale-stable weekday formatting)

**Verification**
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm test` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅
- Branch rebased onto latest `upstream/main` to keep the PR diff minimal and clean.

**Backward Compatibility**
- No behavior change for valid `exec`/`bash` calls.
- Invalid calls with empty/missing `command` are now rejected early with an explicit reason (intentional).

**Risk / Rollback**
- Risk: any external workflow relying on empty `exec` calls (considered invalid input) will now be blocked.
- Rollback: revert the pre-call guard and circuit-breaker additions; UI locale stabilization can be reverted independently.

**Checklist**
- [x] Focused PR scope (exec safety + error circuit breaker; UI is test-stability)
- [x] Up-to-date with upstream main
- [x] check/test/build passing
- [x] No secrets added

target.title: fix(gateway): add .catch() to SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 signal handlers
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The SIGTERM and SIGUSR1 signal handlers in the gateway run-loop use `void (async () => { ... })()` to dynamically import the lifecycle module, but neither has a `.catch()` handler on the resulting promise.
- Why it matters: If `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule()` rejects (disk full, OOM, module corruption), `request()` is never called and the gateway hangs in a half-alive state with no error logged.
- What changed: Added `.catch()` with `gatewayLog.error()` to both handlers. The SIGTERM catch also calls `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` as a fallback so the gateway always completes shutdown.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the lifecycle module itself, signal registration, or restart logic. Only the error-handling wrapper around the existing async IIFE.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83116
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Unhandled promise rejection when dynamic import fails inside SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 async signal handlers.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `runGatewayLoop` function via vitest module mocking. The gateway startup, signal dispatching, and logger are mocked, but the signal handler logic (including the new `.catch` paths) runs for real. When the handler's async body is configured to reject, the real catch handler runs and calls `gatewayLog.error`.
- Tests added and passing (30 total, 2 new):
  ```
  ✓ catches SIGTERM handler errors, logs them, and falls back to stop (#83131)
  ✓ catches SIGUSR1 handler errors and logs them without crashing (#83131)
  ```
- SIGTERM test: Mocks the dynamic import inside the handler to reject with `"dynamic import failed"`. Verifies `gatewayLog.error` is called with `"failed to handle SIGTERM: Error: dynamic import failed"` and `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` is invoked as fallback, producing graceful exit code 0.
- SIGUSR1 test: Mocks the handler to reject with `"sigusr1 lifecycle import failed"`. Verifies `gatewayLog.error` is called with the error message and the server is NOT closed (error is logged, process stays alive).
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — 30 passed, 0 failed.
- What was not tested: Live gateway process receiving real SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 signals with a broken dynamic import.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The `void (async () => { ... })()` pattern intentionally discards the promise, but without `.catch()` any rejection is silently swallowed. This is safe when the async body cannot throw, but `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule()` performs a dynamic import that can reject under resource pressure.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No eslint `no-floating-promises` coverage for this pattern since the `void` prefix satisfies the rule.
- Contributing context (if known): The SIGUSR1 handler was extended multiple times with new lifecycle logic, each time adding to the async body without reconsidering error handling.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: SIGTERM handler still calls `request("stop")` when dynamic import rejects.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: A seam test can mock `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule` to reject and verify `request()` is called.
- If no new test is added, why not: The run-loop test suite already covers signal handling; the `.catch()` is a defensive addition that is difficult to trigger without mocking the module loader.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This only affects error handling for an edge case (dynamic import failure during signal handling) that previously resulted in a silent hang.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
SIGTERM -> async import -> reject -> (promise swallowed) -> gateway hangs

After:
SIGTERM -> async import -> reject -> .catch() -> log error + request("stop") -> clean shutdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22.15.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Default gateway config

### Steps

1. Start an OpenClaw gateway
2. Simulate SIGTERM when `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule()` would reject (e.g., corrupt module cache)
3. Observe that the gateway now logs the error and initiates shutdown

### Expected

- Error is logged via `gatewayLog.error()`
- `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` is called as fallback
- Gateway shuts down cleanly

### Actual

- Before: Gateway hangs silently with no error output
- After: Error logged, shutdown initiated via fallback

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Simulated dynamic import rejection in both SIGTERM and SIGUSR1 handlers; confirmed error logging and fallback behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Verified that successful signal handling (no import failure) is unchanged by the added `.catch()`.
- What you did **not** verify: Actual OOM/disk-full conditions on a running gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The SIGTERM fallback calls `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` without consuming the restart intent, so a restart-via-SIGTERM that fails on import would fall back to stop instead of restart.
  - Mitigation: This is the safer default. A failed module load means we cannot safely determine restart intent, so stopping is correct. The error is logged for debugging.

target.title: fix(canvas): prefer path-scoped capability tokens
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Canvas path-scoped capability URLs could keep a stale `oc_cap` query parameter after Gateway URL normalization.
- Solution: make the path-scoped capability token authoritative when both path and query tokens are present.
- What changed: Gateway normalization now overwrites stale query-string `oc_cap` values with the path token, and injected Canvas live-reload code reads a scoped path token before falling back to the query string.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): capability minting, TTLs, route authorization, Canvas file serving, and the node-host Canvas implementation are unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Canvas pages can be opened through path-scoped capability URLs. If the same URL also carries an older `oc_cap` query parameter, Gateway HTTP handling and the injected live-reload WebSocket path can disagree about which token is current. The path-scoped token is the scoped authority in that URL shape, so it should replace stale query fallback state consistently.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Canvas capability handling now prefers the current path-scoped token over a stale query-string `oc_cap`.
- Real environment tested: local macOS development checkout, branch `canvas-capability`, head `c577a4d3cc`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Started a real local Canvas host with live reload enabled.
  - Normalized a path-scoped Canvas URL with a conflicting stale query token using the Gateway capability normalizer.
  - Fetched the rewritten Canvas URL from the local Canvas host.
  - Confirmed the served HTML includes the injected path-token parser and path-first fallback.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/plugin-node-capability.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts extensions/canvas/src/host/server.test.ts`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/canvas/src/host/a2ui-shared.ts extensions/canvas/src/host/server.test.ts src/gateway/plugin-node-capability.ts src/gateway/plugin-node-capability.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied local Canvas host and normalizer output:

```text
[runtime] canvas host listening on http://127.0.0.1:<port> (root <temp-canvas-root>)
[proof] normalized capability=current-token
[proof] normalized pathname=/__openclaw__/canvas/
[proof] rewritten url=/__openclaw__/canvas/?oc_cap=current-token&download=1
[proof] canvas http status=200
[proof] served canvas body=present
[proof] injected path capability parser=present
[proof] injected path-first fallback=present
```

- Observed result after fix: a request path shaped like `/__openclaw__/cap/current-token/__openclaw__/canvas/?oc_cap=stale-token&download=1` normalizes to capability `current-token`; the rewritten Canvas URL carries `oc_cap=current-token`, not the stale query token; the live Canvas host serves the rewritten Canvas URL successfully; the injected live-reload script reads the scoped path token before query-string fallback.
- What was not tested: full browser WebSocket live-reload round trip. The proof used the real Canvas host HTTP path and the Gateway capability normalizer, then verified the injected live-reload script in the served HTML.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this change, URL normalization preserved an existing stale `oc_cap` query value even when the path-scoped token was newer.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: scoped capability URL normalization only added `oc_cap` when the query parameter was absent, and the injected Canvas script only read `oc_cap` from `location.search`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test for path-scoped capability URLs that also contain a stale query-string token.
- Contributing context: path-scoped URLs and query-string fallback both exist for compatibility, but the path-scoped token is the current scoped authority.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/plugin-node-capability.test.ts`
  - `extensions/canvas/src/host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test locks in: a path-scoped capability token replaces a stale `oc_cap` query token, and injected Canvas live-reload code reads the path token before query fallback.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is local to URL normalization and Canvas live-reload injection.
- Existing test that already covers this: none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Canvas pages served from path-scoped capability URLs keep HTTP and live-reload WebSocket capability handling aligned when a stale `oc_cap` query parameter is present.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
/__openclaw__/cap/current-token/__openclaw__/canvas/?oc_cap=stale-token
  -> /__openclaw__/canvas/?oc_cap=stale-token

After:
/__openclaw__/cap/current-token/__openclaw__/canvas/?oc_cap=stale-token
  -> /__openclaw__/canvas/?oc_cap=current-token
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

1. Start a local Canvas host with live reload enabled.

3. Fetch the rewritten Canvas URL from the local Canvas host.
4. Verify the served HTML includes the path-token parser and path-first fallback.
5. Run targeted regression tests and formatting checks.

### Expected

- The path-scoped token is authoritative.
- The rewritten URL uses `oc_cap=current-token`.
- The injected live-reload script reads the path-scoped token before query fallback.

### Actual

- Verified scenarios: stale query token is replaced by the path token; local Canvas host serves the rewritten URL; injected live-reload script includes the path-token parser and path-first fallback.

target.title: fix: add x-openclaw-session header for stable agent-scoped HTTP sessions
target.body:
## Summary

Partially addresses #68920 (the QMD re-index overhead component).

Every `/v1/chat/completions` call that omits the OpenAI `user` field receives a fresh random-UUID session key. This triggers a full QMD re-index on each request, which accounts for a significant portion of the 10-15s TTFB reported by voice/realtime callers (LiveKit, Twilio, etc.).

Voice stacks cannot always inject the `user` field per-request. This PR adds a new `x-openclaw-session` header that produces a **deterministic, agent-scoped session key** without clobbering the existing full-override behaviour of `x-openclaw-session-key`.

## Priority order (unchanged except for the new entry)

| Priority | Source | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x-openclaw-session-key` header | Explicit full override (unchanged) |
| 2 | `user` request field | Agent-scoped (unchanged) |
| 3 | `x-openclaw-session` header (**new**) | Agent-scoped stable hint |
| 4 | Random UUID | Per-call fallback (unchanged) |

## Usage

Voice/realtime callers can now pass a stable call identifier:

```
POST /v1/chat/completions
x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123
```

The gateway resolves this to `{prefix}-session:livekit-room-abc123` scoped to the agent, so warm-session cache hits apply and QMD sync is skipped on subsequent requests in the same call.

## What this does NOT fix

The core 15-20K-token framework system prompt assembly still runs on every request. A `lightContext` / `x-openclaw-light-context` mode that bypasses the full context pipeline is a separate, larger architectural change.

## Test plan

- [ ] Without `x-openclaw-session`: behaviour unchanged — new UUID session per call
- [ ] With `x-openclaw-session: my-id`: same stable session key across calls (verify no QMD re-index on second call)
- [ ] `user` field still takes priority over `x-openclaw-session`
- [ ] `x-openclaw-session-key` still takes highest priority, overriding both
- [ ] Two different agents with the same `x-openclaw-session` value get different session keys (agent scoping confirmed)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `/v1/chat/completions` calls without the OpenAI `user` field each received a fresh random UUID session key, triggering a full QMD re-index on every request and inflating TTFB to 10-15s for voice/realtime callers.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (`openclaw start`), `gateway.http.endpoints.chatCompletions.enabled: true`, macOS 14.7, Node 22.4, auth mode `token`.

**Exact steps or command run after fix:**

```bash
# Call 1 — pass x-openclaw-session hint
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
  -w "\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\n"

# Call 2 — same hint, expect warm-session hit
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
  -w "\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\n"
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Terminal capture — gateway debug log showing session key resolution and TTFB:

```
# --- before patch (no header) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai:9f4a1b2c-3d5e-4f67-8901-ab2cd3ef4567  (cold)
[gateway] QMD re-index started
TTFB: 11.432s

# --- after patch, call 1 (cold) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123  (cold)
[gateway] QMD re-index started
TTFB: 10.891s

# --- after patch, call 2 (same header, warm) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123  (warm hit)
[gateway] QMD sync skipped — session already indexed
TTFB: 0.347s
```

**Observed result after fix:** Calls 1 and 2 with the same `x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123` header resolve to the identical session key `agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123`. Call 2 skips QMD re-index and returns in ~350ms vs ~11s for the cold call. Without the header the key stays random-UUID and every call is cold.

**What was not tested:** Load behaviour under concurrent voice sessions; LiveKit/Twilio SDK integration beyond curl.

target.title: Fix: apply humanDelay between Telegram streamed block replies (Resolves #68945)
target.body:
Fixes #68945.

The Telegram block-streaming path does not wire `resolveHumanDelayConfig(cfg, agentId)` into `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher`, so `agents.defaults.humanDelay` / per-agent `humanDelay` overrides have no effect on Telegram block replies.

This change matches the pattern already in Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, Tlon, and Mattermost: import `resolveHumanDelayConfig` through the existing `bot-message-dispatch.agent.runtime.ts` seam and pass its result as `dispatcherOptions.humanDelay` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` (74 passed, including new `forwards configured humanDelay to the block dispatcher` case)

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: fix: redact Docker setup gateway token output
target.body:
## Summary

- Stops `scripts/docker/setup.sh` from printing the raw `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` during Docker setup.
- Replaces the setup summary token output with a short SHA-256 fingerprint and token length.
- Prints the `.env` token file path and an explicit retrieval command for operators who need to run the authenticated health check manually.
- Adds regression coverage in `test/scripts/test-install-sh-docker.test.ts`.

## Motivation

The Docker setup flow treated the gateway token as ordinary operator output. Because setup output can land in CI logs, shell transcripts, or shared bastion logs, printing the raw bearer token could turn logs into reusable gateway credentials.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84468
- Related #84469
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Docker setup no longer prints the full gateway bearer token to stdout/stderr.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto current `upstream/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs 'test/scripts/test-install-sh-docker.test.ts' && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs 'test/scripts/test-install-sh-docker.test.ts' && pnpm tsgo`
- Evidence after fix: oxlint reported 0 warnings/errors, the focused Vitest file passed 17 tests, and `pnpm tsgo` completed successfully.
- Observed result after fix: the Docker setup script now prints token fingerprints and token-file retrieval guidance instead of the raw token.
- What was not tested: End-to-end Docker startup on a Docker-capable host.
- Before evidence: The issue body records the previous raw `Gateway token: $OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`, `Token: $OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`, and inline health command token output.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `scripts/docker/setup.sh` echoed the resolved gateway token directly in setup status output and embedded it in a printed health-check command.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Docker setup tests did not assert that token-related setup output avoids raw token interpolation.
- Contributing context (if known): The script offered convenient copy/paste operator output without distinguishing secrets from non-sensitive setup details.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this: script-level regression coverage for Docker setup output.
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/test-install-sh-docker.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: the script must use the fingerprint helper, must print fingerprint/token-file guidance, and must not contain the reported raw token echo statements or raw-token health command echo.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it checks the exact setup script output paths and executes the shell fingerprint helper without requiring a live Docker daemon.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None identified.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Docker setup output now shows `Gateway token fingerprint:` and `Token fingerprint:` instead of the raw gateway token.
- Docker setup output now includes `Token file: $ENV_FILE` and a command that retrieves the token from `.env` only when the operator explicitly runs it.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Gateway token handling changed only in setup output. The token remains available from `.env` for authorized local operators, while routine logs no longer disclose the bearer secret.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

### Actual

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs 'test/scripts/test-install-sh-docker.test.ts'`: passed, 17 tests.

target.title: feat(memory): switch default local embedding model to bge-m3 Q8_0 🤖 AI-assisted
target.body:
## What

Replaces the default local embedding model for memory search from `embeddinggemma-300M-qat` (768 dims) to BAAI/bge-m3 Q8\_0 via `ggml-org/bge-m3-Q8_0-GGUF` (1024 dims).

## Why

With the current `embeddinggemma-300M` model, memory search frequently misses results that are conceptually related but lexically distant from the query — for example, searching for a topic using different wording than what was written in the indexed content. This is a known limitation of smaller token-matching-oriented models.

`bge-m3` (BAAI, 570M parameters, BERT-family architecture) ranks substantially higher on MTEB retrieval benchmarks and produces better cluster separation for semantically related but lexically distinct content. It is also multilingual, which benefits non-English memory content.

The download size is ~567 MB (Q8\_0) vs ~300 MB for embeddinggemma, which is a reasonable trade-off for the quality improvement.

## Migration

The existing atomic reindex path (`runAtomicFullReindex` + `ensureVectorTable`) already handles dimension changes (768→1024) automatically — it detects the dim mismatch, drops and recreates `chunks_vec`, and reindexes all chunks. No manual migration is required.

Users upgrading will trigger a one-time full reindex on next `openclaw memory index --force` (or on next startup with `onSessionStart` enabled). The embedding cache will naturally miss on the new model, forcing fresh embeddings.

## Changes

- `src/memory/embeddings.ts`: updated `DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL`, exported it for testability
- `src/memory/embeddings.test.ts`: added regression assertion for the model identifier

target.title: Strip apiKey fields from generated models.json
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: generated `models.json` could persist `apiKey` fields from provider config rows.
- Solution: strip `apiKey` recursively from the final `models.json` write payload.
- What changed: provider/model metadata still writes, but provider-level and nested `apiKey` fields are omitted; tests now assert this contract.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): runtime auth resolution and provider discovery credential lookup remain outside the generated file.

## Motivation

- Prevent model config auth material from being persisted in a file that agent/runtime readers may inspect.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #11829
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: generated `models.json` no longer includes provider-level or nested `apiKey` fields that could be exposed to agent/runtime readers.
- Real environment tested: local Windows checkout using Node v24.14.0 from the Codex bundled runtime because the system Node v22.15.0 is below the repo requirement.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the real `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` path against a temporary OpenClaw agent dir with a configured provider containing fake provider-level and model-level `apiKey` values, then read the generated `models.json`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live terminal output:

```text
models_path=C:\Users\CAMILO\Documents\Codex\2026-05-20\you-are-my-autonomous-public-work\openclaw\.artifacts\proof-agent\models.json
provider_present=true
provider_apiKey_present=false
model_apiKey_present=false
secret_string_present=false
model_secret_string_present=false
model_id=proof-model
```

- Observed result after fix: provider rows, base URLs, headers, and model metadata still write normally, while `apiKey` is omitted from `models.json`.
- What was not tested: full repository test suite and live end-to-end agent prompt inspection.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): not captured for this generated-file security hardening path; after-fix copied live output above shows the generated file no longer contains provider or model apiKey fields.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `planOpenClawModelsJsonWithDeps` serialized the fully merged provider config directly, including auth fields that were needed for discovery/runtime setup but not safe to persist in `models.json`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests asserted auth markers were persisted instead of asserting generated-file redaction.
- Contributing context (if known): the existing pipeline already normalized several secret references, but final write serialization had no dedicated `apiKey` removal step.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts`, `src/agents/models-config.runtime-source-snapshot.test.ts`, `src/agents/models-config.skips-writing-models-json-no-env-token.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: generated `models.json` keeps provider/model metadata but omits provider-level and nested `apiKey` fields.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the write-plan output directly and existing generated-file cases that previously expected auth markers.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Generated `models.json` no longer contains `apiKey` properties.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes, `apiKey` fields are stripped from generated `models.json`.
- New/changed network calls? No

## Verification

- `C:\Users\CAMILO\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe scripts\run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/models-config.runtime-source-snapshot.test.ts src/agents/models-config.skips-writing-models-json-no-env-token.test.ts --run`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: Doctor: convert interactive maintenance checks
target.body:
## Goal

Establish the structured doctor repair contract and apply it to the first interactive-maintenance repairs without changing the user-facing order of `openclaw doctor`.

The core goal is not just moving code. It is making doctor repairs observable and auditable through one contract:

```ts
detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[] | { findings, notes }
repair(ctx, findings) -> HealthRepairResult
```

`detect()` is the read-only source of truth for normal doctor diagnostics, `doctor --lint`, and post-repair validation. `repair()` is the only mutating structured path, receives the exact findings from detect, and reports what it changed through `changes`, `warnings`, `notes`, `diffs`, and `effects`.

## Scope

| Area | Goal | Behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Shell completion | Make completion repair detectable, previewable, and validated. | Detect reports stale/missing cached completion state; repair updates completion state; detect-after proves it is no longer reported. |
| Startup channel maintenance | Keep startup-channel repair at its original contribution point while using the structured runner. | Existing contribution order is preserved; repair result is reported through the shared contract. |
| Systemd linger | Represent guided service/user-manager repair as side effects and ordered notes. | Detect is silent/read-only; repair can present operator context before prompting; service effects are explicit. |
| Browser profile residue | Preserve prior repair behavior while validating it through the health-check contract. | Repair is followed by scoped detect-after validation. |

## Contract Table

| Contract piece | Requirement | Why it matters |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Read-only detect | No mutation and no terminal printing from structured detect. | Normal doctor, lint, and detect-after can reuse the same source of truth. |
| Findings as repair input | Repair receives the findings it is allowed to fix. | Prevents repair from drifting from what doctor detected. |
| Detection notes | Informational doctor notes stay separate from lint findings. | `doctor --lint` remains signal-bearing instead of noisy. |
| Immediate repair notes | Timing-sensitive repair presentation flows through `ctx.doctor.repairNote(...)` / `DoctorPrompter.note(...)` and is not replayed as deferred output. | Helpers can preserve prompt context without duplicate terminal notes. |
| Deferred repair notes | `repair().notes` is for notes the doctor layer prints after repair returns, before generic change/warning summaries. | Non-interactive result presentation stays ordered and testable. |
| Dry-run/effects | Preview reports concrete `changes[]`, `diffs[]`, or `effects[]`; no fake diffs. | Reviewers can see what would happen before mutation. |
| Detect-after | Non-dry-run repairs are validated by running detect again on the repaired scope. | A repair is not considered successful just because it emitted an effect. |

## Proof

| Proof | Result |
| --- | --- |
| Focused Vitest coverage for completion, systemd linger, startup-channel maintenance, browser residue, core checks, health contributions, repair flow, and adjacent doctor prompter consumers | 14 focused files / 164 tests passed on squashed head. |
| Core prod TypeScript config | Passed. |
| Core test TypeScript config | Passed. |
| Extension prod/test TypeScript configs and package-boundary check | Passed before squash; package-boundary also passed on GitHub before the squash-only history rewrite. |
| Core oxlint and extension channel/bundled lint checks | Passed before squash; old failing GitHub lint boxes passed before the squash-only history rewrite. |
| Build artifacts / plugin SDK strict-smoke path | Passed before squash; `build-artifacts` passed on GitHub before the squash-only history rewrite. |
| Guard checks exercised locally | Conflict markers, tool display, host env policy, duplicate coverage, package patches, auth pairing guards, webhook body-order guard, and import cycles passed. |
| Final autoreview on squashed diff | Passed with no accepted/actionable findings. The helper also ran `pnpm run check` successfully. |
| Disposable Azure Ubuntu proof | Shell completion and systemd linger showed before-detect, repair, and after-detect success on a real user systemd manager. |

Proof artifacts recorded in the PR:

- Azure CLI transcript: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83753-isomorphism/pr-83753/azure-cli-proof-8cef0bb/azure-doctor-cli-proof.redacted.log
- Azure proof excerpt: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83753-isomorphism/pr-83753/azure-cli-proof-8cef0bb/azure-proof-excerpt.txt
- Azure proof summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83753-isomorphism/pr-83753/azure-cli-proof-8cef0bb/summary.redacted.txt
- Focused test transcript: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83753-isomorphism/pr-83753/real-proof-8cef0bb/proof.clean.log

## Out of Scope

- Exposing new public doctor dry-run/diff flags.
- Converting every doctor repair bucket in one PR.
- Replacing owner-specific repair policy with generic core policy.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Converted doctor maintenance repairs run through structured health checks at their original doctor contribution positions, while preserving user-facing repair behavior and adding scoped post-repair validation.

Real environment tested: Current squashed PR head `6950cc4839f377ce22243491c4689a4a2ffc1bc07` was tested with focused source-checkout tests, core prod/test TypeScript checks, final autoreview, and an accidental broad `pnpm run check` from the autoreview helper. Earlier pre-squash head also passed local CI-equivalent type/lint/build/package-boundary shards and GitHub’s previously failing boxes. Prior disposable Azure Ubuntu 24.04 proof covered a real user systemd manager and real `loginctl` linger state.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: focused Vitest files for the converted checks and adjacent prompter consumers; core prod/test tsgo; `git diff --check`; `autoreview --mode branch`; and, through the helper, `pnpm run check`. Earlier: extension prod/test tsgo, package-boundary compile/canary, core oxlint, extension channel/bundled lint, build artifacts, guard checks, and Azure `doctor --lint` / `doctor --fix --yes` against disposable shell-completion and systemd-linger states.

Evidence after fix: focused tests, core typecheck, whitespace check, final autoreview, broad local check, previous CI-equivalent shard proof, previous GitHub old-failure boxes, and Azure before/after CLI proof passed.

Observed result after fix: shell-completion and systemd-linger lint failed before repair, `doctor --fix` succeeded, lint passed after repair, and immediate repair notes are no longer replayed as duplicate deferred notes.

What was not tested: Docker was used only for the non-privileged shell-completion repro. Privileged systemd/loginctl behavior was proven on Azure instead of Docker because a real Linux user manager is more representative. Fresh GitHub CI for squashed head `6950cc4839f377ce22243491c4689a4a2ffc1bc07` was queued at PR update time.


## Follow-on Examples

These follow-on PRs apply this contract to the next doctor repair buckets and serve as concrete examples for future conversions.

| PR | Bucket | Structured repair examples | Proof shape |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| #84290 | Plugin registry and configured plugin installs | Config/file/package/state repairs; real manifest diffs; configured plugin release metadata; registry/package effects without fake diffs. | Focused detect/repair/detect-after tests, dry-run/effects assertions, core typecheck, autoreview, earlier Crabbox CLI proof. |
| #84326 | Sandbox registry, images, and scope diagnostics | State migration effects; process effects for buildable sandbox images; operator-managed custom image skips; read-only sandbox scope findings. | Focused sandbox/core/lint tests, dry-run no-mutation checks, detect-after validation, core typecheck, autoreview. |
| #84340 | Gateway service cleanup and config drift | Service-manager effects for launchd/systemd work; silent detect/lint; repair-time notes through `DoctorPrompter.note(...)`; no synthetic service file diffs. | Focused gateway/core/lint tests, note-order proof, service effect assertions, core typecheck, autoreview. |
| #84366 | Session locks and session transcripts | Finding-scoped file mutation; dry-run previews; stale lock removal; transcript active-branch rewrite; adjacent stale/broken files left untouched. | Temp-state filesystem tests, scoped mutation tests, detect-after validation, core typecheck, autoreview. |
| #84450 | Config audit and cron-store cleanup | Config-audit and legacy cron-store repairs report concrete file/config effects, dry-run changes, and real diffs when the repair can express one. | Focused config-audit/cron tests, dry-run no-mutation assertions, detect-after cleanup validation, core typecheck, autoreview. |
| #84472 | Top-level doctor preview surface | `doctor --dry-run`, `--diff`, and JSON preview aggregate structured findings, notes, effects, diffs, and explicit skipped unconverted contributions without adding any oc-path dependency or synthesized oc-path output. | Focused CLI/doctor/contribution tests, JSON/no-mutation proof, oc-path grep proof, touched-file oxlint, test-src typecheck, Codex review. |

target.title: Agents: add claude-sdk runtime with native-tool bridge + Phase 3 default flip
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `claude-sdk` agent runtime driver backed by `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`, alongside the existing `embedded` (pi-ai) and `acp` drivers, and flips the default to `claude-sdk` (Phase 3).

This is a re-submission of the original #68135 work, rebased onto current `main` (2420+ upstream commits of drift) with review-feedback fixes included.

- **Runtime dispatch** — `src/agents/runtime-dispatch.ts` + `src/agents/claude-sdk/*` introduces the `runAgent` entry point that picks the active driver from `agents.list[<id>].runtime.type`. Default flips to `claude-sdk`; `embedded` kept explicit for the compat window.
- **Native-tool bridge** — in-process MCP server exposes OpenClaw-native tools (`message`, `sessions.send`, `cron.add`, plugin-contributed) to the SDK via TypeBox→Zod schema conversion (`src/agents/claude-sdk/typebox-to-zod.ts` + `tools-adapter.ts`).
- **Hooks adapter** — translates OpenClaw hook events to SDK equivalents; honors `HookInvocationPolicy.enabled` to skip disabled hooks (parity with embedded runtime).
- **Credential modes** — `credential: "subscription" | "profile"` knob defaults to `subscription` so requests use the user's Claude.ai login rather than a metered API key.
- **Session-mirror** — writes SDK turns to a sidecar file so pi-ai SessionManager's truncation race doesn't clobber our transcript.
- **Tool-deny enforcement** — `tools.deny` + `tools.byProvider.anthropic.deny` now scope SDK built-ins (Bash, Read, Edit, Grep, Glob) via the SDK's `disallowedTools` option (matches how embedded filters native tools).

## Plugin SDK surface

- `runAgent` export from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/agent-runtime` for stable runtime-driver dispatch
- `RunAgentResult` / `RunAgentParams` types exported from the same surface
- Legacy `runEmbeddedPiAgent` export marked `@deprecated`; new plugins should migrate to `runAgent`

## Skill → sub-agent generator

target.title: docs(providers): merge GLM docs into Z.AI page
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Z.AI and GLM are actually the same provider in OpenClaw (`zai` provider, `ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions / Bearer auth), but the docs were split across `/providers/zai` (provider page) and `/providers/glm` (model-family page). The two pages duplicated setup guidance, drifted apart over time, and users entering from either side had to bounce between them to get the full picture.
- **Solution:** Merge the GLM page into `docs/providers/zai.md`, delete `docs/providers/glm.md`, and point every internal link / nav entry / i18n nav / redirect at `/providers/zai`. While merging, add a Property table and a Config example to the Z.AI page, and simplify Getting started from 3 steps to 2 (onboarding already sets the default model, so the "Set a default model" step is no longer needed).
- **What changed:**
  - `docs/providers/zai.md`: add Property table; add Config example section; correct the auto-detect wording (the `zai-api-key` choice **probes** supported endpoints with the API key — it does not infer from a key prefix); simplify Getting started from 3 to 2 steps; align Related cards with Model selection / Configuration reference.
  - Delete `docs/providers/glm.md` (content merged into `zai.md`). The legacy env var `Z_AI_API_KEY` (which the runtime still accepts via `src/infra/env.ts` and lists in `extensions/zai/openclaw.plugin.json`) is preserved in the consolidated docs — see the auth row of the Property table and the Auth details accordion.
  - `docs/docs.json`: change `/glm` destination from `/providers/glm` to `/providers/zai`; add new `/providers/glm` → `/providers/zai` redirect; remove `providers/glm` from navigation.
  - `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`: keep main's `<CardGroup>` structure; rename the GLM card to `Z.AI (GLM)` and update href to `/providers/zai`.
  - `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/providers/models.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`, `docs/providers/anthropic.md`, `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json`: every reference to `glm` / `providers/glm` updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Pure docs. No changes to runtime / `extensions/zai` / `src/plugins/provider-zai-endpoint.ts` / any plugin / CI / config schema. No standalone stub page kept at `/providers/glm` — the redirect in `docs.json` handles it.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

(Docs-only PR. UI / DX is checked because this is the first-touch onboarding doc for the Z.AI/GLM provider and directly shapes the new-user setup flow.)

## Linked Issue/PR

No prior issue — submitted as a direct contribution.

- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The motivation comes from a real onboarding gap: many entry points (`docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`) surface `GLM models` first and route users to `/providers/glm`, which had its own self-contained setup. But the actual OpenClaw provider is `zai` (`ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions), so half of the operational information also lived under `/providers/zai`. Over time the two pages drifted, and "GLM is a model family hosted by Z.AI" — a key conceptual point — was hard to express cleanly across two separate pages. Consolidating to a single Z.AI page makes that relationship explicit at the top of the page and removes a recurring source of new-user confusion. The merge also lets us reflect the current onboarding behavior accurately: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice zai-api-key` (and the regional `zai-coding-*` / `zai-cn` variants) already sets the default model, so the old "Set a default model" doc step is now stale and is removed in this PR.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Doc split between Z.AI and GLM produced two competing entry points and stale links. After the merge, every legacy URL (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) must redirect to `/providers/zai`; the Z.AI page must render the full GLM model catalog and the merged Getting started; nav / providers index must not show a standalone GLM entry.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Mintlify dev preview. `npx --yes mintlify dev` on Linux, Node v24.15.0, started from `docs/` in a clean checkout of this branch. `docs/docs.json` uses Mintlify schema `https://mintlify.com/docs.json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  cd docs
  npx --yes mintlify dev

  # In another terminal, once the preview is ready on localhost:3000:
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):**

  Terminal output from the local Mintlify preview (verbatim):

  ```
  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  HTTP/1.1 200 OK

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  <title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  Coding Plan
  GLM-5
  Z.AI
  glm-4.5
  glm-4.5-air
  glm-4.5-flash
  glm-4.5v
  glm-4.6
  glm-4.6v
  glm-4.7
  glm-4.7-flash
  glm-4.7-flashx
  glm-5
  glm-5-models
  glm-5-turbo
  glm-5.1
  glm-5v-turbo
  glm-models-naming-
  z.ai

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  6
  ```

  The 6 occurrences of `Z_AI_API_KEY` cover the auth row of the Property table, the "Auth details" accordion bullet, and the rendered React payload — confirming the legacy env var is documented in both prominent spots after the merge.

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - `/glm`, `/providers/glm`, and `/zai` all return `307 Temporary Redirect` with `location: /providers/zai`. (Production Mintlify may use a different redirect status, but the destination is the same and behavior is equivalent.)
  - `/providers/zai` returns `200 OK` with `<title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>`.
  - The rendered HTML contains the full GLM model list (`glm-4.5-air`, `glm-4.5-flash`, `glm-4.7-flash`, `glm-4.7-flashx`, `glm-5-turbo`, `glm-5-models`, `glm-models`) and `Z.AI` / `Coding Plan` sections — the original `glm.md` content has been merged in without loss.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings; all previous links to `providers/glm` have been updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What was not tested:** Production Mintlify CDN cache. If any stale `/providers/glm` cache objects exist on the production deployment, they will either expire naturally or need to be purged on the deployment side — out of scope for this PR.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** The "before" state can be observed on the current production deployment: https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/glm and https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/zai are still two separate pages with duplicated setup that has drifted apart.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Visitors to `/providers/glm` or `/glm` are redirected to `/providers/zai`. (`/glm` previously redirected to `/providers/glm`; the destination is updated. `/providers/glm` is a new redirect.)
- The docs site navigation and providers index no longer show a standalone `GLM models` entry. `Z.AI` is the single entry point and its subtitle mentions GLM.
- The Z.AI page now has a Property table and a Config example. Getting started is simplified from 3 steps (onboard → set default model → verify) to 2 steps (onboard → verify), reflecting that onboarding already sets the default model.
- The endpoint auto-detect description is corrected: the `zai-api-key` choice probes supported endpoints with the API key, rather than inferring from a key prefix.
- The legacy `Z_AI_API_KEY` env var (still accepted by the runtime; documented previously only on the deleted GLM page) is now documented on the consolidated Z.AI page — in the Property table auth row and the Auth details accordion. No behavior change for users who only set `ZAI_API_KEY`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

Docs-only — no runtime / auth / network behavior changes.

## Repro + Verification

See §Real behavior proof above — Environment, Steps, Expected, and Actual are all covered there.

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0; `npx --yes mintlify dev` (no global install)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `docs/docs.json` (Mintlify schema)

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets — see Real behavior proof / Evidence after fix
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — optional; terminal evidence is sufficient
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - All three legacy URLs (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) correctly redirect to `/providers/zai` in the local Mintlify preview.
  - The rendered `/providers/zai` page contains the full GLM model list and the merged Getting started content.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings.
  - Branch hygiene: ahead 1 commit relative to `origin/main`, diff limited to 10 files under `docs/`, no runtime / extensions / UI / superpowers files leaked into the PR.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Both `/glm` (existing redirect, destination updated) and `/providers/glm` (newly added redirect) paths are exercised.
  - The Chinese navigation `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json` also has `zh-CN/providers/glm` removed.
- What was **not** verified:
  - CDN cache purge on the production docs.openclaw.ai deployment (deployment-side concern).
  - Whether every GLM model id string in the page still corresponds to a callable model on Z.AI today — this PR only consolidates docs and does not change the bundled `zai` manifest.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — legacy URLs still resolve via redirects.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: If the production Mintlify deployment has CDN-cached `/providers/glm` responses, users may see stale content briefly.
  - Mitigation: Cached entries will expire naturally, or a maintainer can purge them on the deployment side. The redirect itself is in `docs.json` and takes effect immediately once cache is cleared.
- Risk: External blog posts or old GitHub issues may still link to `/providers/glm`.
  - Mitigation: The `docs.json` redirect covers both `/providers/glm` and `/glm`, so external links continue to land users on the consolidated page. No external-link fix-up needed.

The `qa/convex-credential-broker/convex/tsconfig.json` file contains `/* */` style comments, which cause JSON parsing errors in strict JSON parsers:

```
Expected ',' or '}' after property value in JSON at position 70
```

- Kept all compiler options and configuration intact
- TypeScript still supports the config file without comments

## Testing
- Validated JSON syntax with `jq`
- All compiler options remain unchanged

Fixes the JSON parsing error while maintaining TypeScript configuration.

target.title: fix(canvas-host): dedupe signal-polyfill in a2ui bundle
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: The a2ui bundle ships **two independent copies** of the `signal-polyfill` module — one renamed with a `\$1` suffix (used by `signalstore`/`watcher`/`effect`) and a fresh unsuffixed copy (used by Lit `SignalWatcher` mixin and `instanceof Signal.State/Computed` guards).
- **Why it matters**: Each copy has its own closure-scoped `activeConsumer`, `SIGNAL`/`NODE` symbols, and `REACTIVE_NODE`. So when a `SignalWatcher` component reads signalstore-backed state, the dependency is recorded on the *suffixed copy's* consumer list — but `SignalWatcher` only sets the *unsuffixed* `activeConsumer`. Result: components render once and never re-render on mutation. The `instanceof Signal.State/Computed` directive guard is also dead code because the classes belong to a different prototype chain.
- **What changed**: Added `signal-polyfill` (and `signal-polyfill/`) to `resolve.alias` in `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tools/CanvasA2UI/rolldown.config.mjs` so both `@lit-labs/signals` and `signal-utils` resolve to the same singleton at bundle time. Regenerated `src/canvas-host/a2ui/a2ui.bundle.js` via `pnpm canvas:a2ui:bundle` and refreshed `src/canvas-host/a2ui/.bundle.hash`.
- **What did NOT change**: No source changes outside the rolldown alias and the regenerated bundle artifacts. No package.json edits, no API surface changes.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Root Cause

The rolldown config aliased `@lit-labs/signals`, `lit`, and `signal-utils/`, but not `signal-polyfill` itself. Rolldown then resolved `signal-polyfill` independently from each consumer's nearest `node_modules` and emitted two module instances. With `treeshake: false`, both instances were retained side-by-side and one received the `\$1` rename to avoid identifier collision.

## Verification

- `pnpm check` (tsgo + lint + import-cycle + madge): green.
- Bundle inspection: `grep -E '^(let|const) (activeConsumer|SIGNAL|REACTIVE_NODE)\b'` returns 3 unsuffixed defs and **0** suffixed defs (was 3+3 before).
- Bundle size: 503,598 → 486,000 bytes.
- `Signal.State`/`Signal.Computed`/`instanceof Signal` references all resolve to the single namespace.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
