target.title: docs(providers): merge GLM docs into Z.AI page
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Z.AI and GLM are actually the same provider in OpenClaw (`zai` provider, `ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions / Bearer auth), but the docs were split across `/providers/zai` (provider page) and `/providers/glm` (model-family page). The two pages duplicated setup guidance, drifted apart over time, and users entering from either side had to bounce between them to get the full picture.
- **Solution:** Merge the GLM page into `docs/providers/zai.md`, delete `docs/providers/glm.md`, and point every internal link / nav entry / i18n nav / redirect at `/providers/zai`. While merging, add a Property table and a Config example to the Z.AI page, and simplify Getting started from 3 steps to 2 (onboarding already sets the default model, so the "Set a default model" step is no longer needed).
- **What changed:**
  - `docs/providers/zai.md`: add Property table; add Config example section; correct the auto-detect wording (the `zai-api-key` choice **probes** supported endpoints with the API key — it does not infer from a key prefix); simplify Getting started from 3 to 2 steps; align Related cards with Model selection / Configuration reference.
  - Delete `docs/providers/glm.md` (content merged into `zai.md`). The legacy env var `Z_AI_API_KEY` (which the runtime still accepts via `src/infra/env.ts` and lists in `extensions/zai/openclaw.plugin.json`) is preserved in the consolidated docs — see the auth row of the Property table and the Auth details accordion.
  - `docs/docs.json`: change `/glm` destination from `/providers/glm` to `/providers/zai`; add new `/providers/glm` → `/providers/zai` redirect; remove `providers/glm` from navigation.
  - `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`: keep main's `<CardGroup>` structure; rename the GLM card to `Z.AI (GLM)` and update href to `/providers/zai`.
  - `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/providers/models.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`, `docs/providers/anthropic.md`, `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json`: every reference to `glm` / `providers/glm` updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Pure docs. No changes to runtime / `extensions/zai` / `src/plugins/provider-zai-endpoint.ts` / any plugin / CI / config schema. No standalone stub page kept at `/providers/glm` — the redirect in `docs.json` handles it.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

(Docs-only PR. UI / DX is checked because this is the first-touch onboarding doc for the Z.AI/GLM provider and directly shapes the new-user setup flow.)

## Linked Issue/PR

No prior issue — submitted as a direct contribution.

- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The motivation comes from a real onboarding gap: many entry points (`docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`) surface `GLM models` first and route users to `/providers/glm`, which had its own self-contained setup. But the actual OpenClaw provider is `zai` (`ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions), so half of the operational information also lived under `/providers/zai`. Over time the two pages drifted, and "GLM is a model family hosted by Z.AI" — a key conceptual point — was hard to express cleanly across two separate pages. Consolidating to a single Z.AI page makes that relationship explicit at the top of the page and removes a recurring source of new-user confusion. The merge also lets us reflect the current onboarding behavior accurately: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice zai-api-key` (and the regional `zai-coding-*` / `zai-cn` variants) already sets the default model, so the old "Set a default model" doc step is now stale and is removed in this PR.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Doc split between Z.AI and GLM produced two competing entry points and stale links. After the merge, every legacy URL (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) must redirect to `/providers/zai`; the Z.AI page must render the full GLM model catalog and the merged Getting started; nav / providers index must not show a standalone GLM entry.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Mintlify dev preview. `npx --yes mintlify dev` on Linux, Node v24.15.0, started from `docs/` in a clean checkout of this branch. `docs/docs.json` uses Mintlify schema `https://mintlify.com/docs.json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  cd docs
  npx --yes mintlify dev

  # In another terminal, once the preview is ready on localhost:3000:
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):**

  Terminal output from the local Mintlify preview (verbatim):

  ```
  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  HTTP/1.1 200 OK

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  <title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  Coding Plan
  GLM-5
  Z.AI
  glm-4.5
  glm-4.5-air
  glm-4.5-flash
  glm-4.5v
  glm-4.6
  glm-4.6v
  glm-4.7
  glm-4.7-flash
  glm-4.7-flashx
  glm-5
  glm-5-models
  glm-5-turbo
  glm-5.1
  glm-5v-turbo
  glm-models-naming-
  z.ai

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  6
  ```

  The 6 occurrences of `Z_AI_API_KEY` cover the auth row of the Property table, the "Auth details" accordion bullet, and the rendered React payload — confirming the legacy env var is documented in both prominent spots after the merge.

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - `/glm`, `/providers/glm`, and `/zai` all return `307 Temporary Redirect` with `location: /providers/zai`. (Production Mintlify may use a different redirect status, but the destination is the same and behavior is equivalent.)
  - `/providers/zai` returns `200 OK` with `<title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>`.
  - The rendered HTML contains the full GLM model list (`glm-4.5-air`, `glm-4.5-flash`, `glm-4.7-flash`, `glm-4.7-flashx`, `glm-5-turbo`, `glm-5-models`, `glm-models`) and `Z.AI` / `Coding Plan` sections — the original `glm.md` content has been merged in without loss.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings; all previous links to `providers/glm` have been updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What was not tested:** Production Mintlify CDN cache. If any stale `/providers/glm` cache objects exist on the production deployment, they will either expire naturally or need to be purged on the deployment side — out of scope for this PR.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** The "before" state can be observed on the current production deployment: https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/glm and https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/zai are still two separate pages with duplicated setup that has drifted apart.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Visitors to `/providers/glm` or `/glm` are redirected to `/providers/zai`. (`/glm` previously redirected to `/providers/glm`; the destination is updated. `/providers/glm` is a new redirect.)
- The docs site navigation and providers index no longer show a standalone `GLM models` entry. `Z.AI` is the single entry point and its subtitle mentions GLM.
- The Z.AI page now has a Property table and a Config example. Getting started is simplified from 3 steps (onboard → set default model → verify) to 2 steps (onboard → verify), reflecting that onboarding already sets the default model.
- The endpoint auto-detect description is corrected: the `zai-api-key` choice probes supported endpoints with the API key, rather than inferring from a key prefix.
- The legacy `Z_AI_API_KEY` env var (still accepted by the runtime; documented previously only on the deleted GLM page) is now documented on the consolidated Z.AI page — in the Property table auth row and the Auth details accordion. No behavior change for users who only set `ZAI_API_KEY`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

Docs-only — no runtime / auth / network behavior changes.

## Repro + Verification

See §Real behavior proof above — Environment, Steps, Expected, and Actual are all covered there.

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0; `npx --yes mintlify dev` (no global install)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `docs/docs.json` (Mintlify schema)

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets — see Real behavior proof / Evidence after fix
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — optional; terminal evidence is sufficient
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - All three legacy URLs (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) correctly redirect to `/providers/zai` in the local Mintlify preview.
  - The rendered `/providers/zai` page contains the full GLM model list and the merged Getting started content.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings.
  - Branch hygiene: ahead 1 commit relative to `origin/main`, diff limited to 10 files under `docs/`, no runtime / extensions / UI / superpowers files leaked into the PR.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Both `/glm` (existing redirect, destination updated) and `/providers/glm` (newly added redirect) paths are exercised.
  - The Chinese navigation `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json` also has `zh-CN/providers/glm` removed.
- What was **not** verified:
  - CDN cache purge on the production docs.openclaw.ai deployment (deployment-side concern).
  - Whether every GLM model id string in the page still corresponds to a callable model on Z.AI today — this PR only consolidates docs and does not change the bundled `zai` manifest.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — legacy URLs still resolve via redirects.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: If the production Mintlify deployment has CDN-cached `/providers/glm` responses, users may see stale content briefly.
  - Mitigation: Cached entries will expire naturally, or a maintainer can purge them on the deployment side. The redirect itself is in `docs.json` and takes effect immediately once cache is cleared.
- Risk: External blog posts or old GitHub issues may still link to `/providers/glm`.
  - Mitigation: The `docs.json` redirect covers both `/providers/glm` and `/glm`, so external links continue to land users on the consolidated page. No external-link fix-up needed.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choice: **`--scope` not `--service`**. This matters because:
- `--scope`: child **inherits the caller's environment** (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved)
- `--service`: child runs in a **clean systemd environment** (would lose all service env vars)

The `env` object is built from `stripSupervisorHintEnv(process.env)` plus update-specific vars, and passed directly to `spawn()` — identical to the existing non-systemd path. Since `--scope` preserves caller env, no `--setenv` forwarding is needed.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 2 new tests: systemd path uses `systemd-run --scope`, non-systemd path uses plain detached node |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-client (7 tests) 640ms
 ✓ gateway-methods (7 tests) 634ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker (node:24-slim, Debian bookworm) for cgroup escape demonstration; macOS arm64 for unit tests.

### Docker proof: process group isolation

```
--- Test A: Non-detached child — same process group → killed together ---
Parent PID=200 PGID=200
Child  PID=208 PGID=200
Same process group: YES
Sending SIGTERM to process group -200...
Child marker: tick 1, tick 2 (only 2 ticks before killed)

--- Test B: Detached child — new process group → survives group kill ---
Parent PID=247 PGID=247
Child  PID=255 PGID=255
Same process group: NO
Sending SIGTERM to process group -247...
Child marker: tick 1-6 + COMPLETED (survived and finished)
```

### Source inspection: env forwarding is correct

```typescript
// Line 343: env built from full process.env
const env = {
    ...stripSupervisorHintEnv(params.env ?? process.env),
    [CONTROL_PLANE_UPDATE_SENTINEL_META_ENV]: metaPath,
    OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF: "1",
};

// Line 352: env passed to spawn() for systemd-run
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
//                                                         ^^^ full env
```

`systemd-run --scope` inherits caller env (unlike `--service` which runs in a clean env). Profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, and package-manager discovery are all preserved.

- **What was not tested**: Live user-systemd update cycle (requires a Linux server with systemd, OpenClaw installed via npm, and a version upgrade available). The cgroup escape mechanism and env forwarding are validated at source and Docker levels.

Closes #84068

target.title: fix(memory): skip placeholder short-term promotions
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a shared short-term promotion guard for markdown placeholder/skeleton snippets.
- Apply it when loading the recall store, recording recall/grounded candidates, ranking, relocating, applying, and rehydrating promotion candidates.
- Add regression coverage for marker-only snippets, heading-plus-empty-bullet skeletons, normalized marker-plus-heading placeholders, stale stored placeholders, and direct apply rejection while preserving ordinary short bullet content.

Fixes #80582.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts -- --runInBand`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Short-term promotion could treat markdown placeholders like `-`, heading-plus-empty-bullet skeletons, and previously persisted normalized skeleton snippets like `- ## Entscheidungen` as durable memory candidates, allowing empty daily-note scaffolding to rank and eventually append to `MEMORY.md`.

Real environment tested: Local filesystem-backed OpenClaw memory workspace on macOS, using PR head `8b3146c4f4`. The standalone Node run created real temp files under `memory/`, wrote `memory/.dreams/short-term-recall.json`, called the production `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` and `applyShortTermPromotions` functions from `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`, and checked the resulting `MEMORY.md` filesystem state.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the repo root, ran `node --import tsx -` with a standalone script that seeds a temp `memory/2026-04-18.md` note plus a persisted short-term recall entry whose snippet is `- ## Entscheidungen`, then invokes production ranking and apply paths with thresholds set to zero so only the placeholder guard can block promotion.

Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from that real temp-workspace run:

```console
$ node --import tsx -
{
  "workspaceFixture": "openclaw-80582-proof-ZyOdqU",
  "storedSnippet": "- ## Entscheidungen",
  "rankedCandidateCount": 0,
  "appliedCount": 0,
  "memoryMdExists": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The normalized stale placeholder `- ## Entscheidungen` was removed from the promotable path before ranking, an explicit apply attempt with the same snippet applied zero promotions, and no `MEMORY.md` file was created. A real bullet containing durable text, `- Keep gateway restarts supervised.`, remains promotable in the regression coverage.

What was not tested: I did not run a full live dreaming cron cycle against a private real workspace; the user-reported failure shape is covered with filesystem-backed promotion fixtures and the standalone temp-workspace OpenClaw promotion run above rather than private memory contents.

target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: Nostr: restore DM delivery path
target.body:
## Summary
- pass a single DM subscription filter object to `subscribeMany()`
- keep `gateway.startAccount()` alive until abort instead of resolving immediately
- add a `sendMedia` fallback so outbound delivery can send attachment links over DM

## Root cause
`main` still had all three failures described in #32252:
1. `nostr-bus.ts` wrapped the DM filter in an array before calling `subscribeMany()`.
2. `channel.ts` resolved `startAccount()` immediately after startup instead of waiting for abort.
3. the outbound adapter only exposed `sendText`, so the generic delivery layer treated Nostr outbound as unconfigured.

## Testing

- `pnpm test extensions/nostr/src/nostr-bus.subscribe.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/nostr/src/channel.test.ts`

target.title: fix(feishu): convert Markdown tables to native table components in cards
target.body:
## Problem

Feishu's Card JSON 2.0 `markdown` element does **not** support standard Markdown table syntax (`| col | col |`). When `buildMarkdownCard()` wraps table-containing text in a single `{"tag": "markdown"}` element, the tables render as broken plain text in Feishu messages.

This means that when `renderMode=card` (or `auto` with detected tables), the card path is taken but tables are unreadable.

### Before (broken)

Tables in card messages appear as raw pipe-separated text:

```
| Name | Score |
|------|-------|
| Alice | 95 |
| Bob | 87 |
```

### After (fixed)

Tables are rendered as native Feishu table components with proper headers, alignment, and row data — matching the official Feishu card design system.

## Solution

Introduces a new `markdown-card.ts` module (`extensions/feishu/src/`) that:

1. **Auto-detects** Markdown tables using a regex that matches header + separator + data rows
2. **Converts** each table to a native Feishu `table` component (Card JSON 2.0) with:
   - Properly typed columns (`display_name`, `data_type`, `width`)
   - Header styling (bold, centered, grey background)
   - Row data mapped to column names
3. **Splits** mixed content into alternating `markdown` + `table` elements
4. **Falls back** gracefully when table parsing fails (keeps raw text)

The existing `buildMarkdownCard()` in `send.ts` now delegates to `buildMarkdownCardWithTables()`, maintaining full backward compatibility.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/feishu/src/markdown-card.ts` | **New** — table detection, parsing, and card builder |
| `extensions/feishu/src/markdown-card.test.ts` | **New** — 19 unit tests |
| `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts` | Updated `buildMarkdownCard()` to use the new builder |
| `extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts` | Added 2 integration tests for `buildMarkdownCard` |

## Test plan

- [x] 19 new unit tests for `parseTableRow`, `mdTableToFeishuTable`, `splitTextAndTables`, `hasMarkdownTables`, `buildMarkdownCardWithTables`
- [x] 2 integration tests for `buildMarkdownCard` (plain text + table conversion)
- [x] All existing `send.test.ts` tests pass (6/6)
- [x] All existing `outbound.test.ts` tests pass (13/13)
- [x] All existing `streaming-card.test.ts` tests pass (7/7)

## References

- [Feishu Card JSON 2.0 Structure](https://open.feishu.cn/document/feishu-cards/card-json-v2-structure)
- [Feishu Markdown Tags](https://open.feishu.cn/document/common-capabilities/message-card/message-cards-content/using-markdown-tags)
- [Feishu Table Component](https://open.feishu.cn/document/feishu-cards/card-json-v2-components/content-components/table)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Docker: Move Playwright browsers to `/opt/playwright`
target.body:
Fix #42819

### Summary
Changed Playwright browser installation path from `~/.cache/ms-playwright` to `/opt/playwright` to align with FHS standards and improve persistence handling.

**Changes:**
- **Dockerfile**:
  - Set `ENV PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH=/opt/playwright`.
  - Pre-create directory with `node:node` ownership (fixes permission errors for runtime installs).
  - Added `/usr/local/bin/chromium` symlink for easier debugging.
- **Docs**: Updated Docker persistence guides to reference `/opt/playwright`.

**Benefits:**
- **FHS Compliance**: Browsers are application dependencies, not temporary cache.
- **Reliability**: Ensures the install directory is always writable by the `node` user, even if the browser isn't pre-installed at build time.
- **Simplicity**: Easier to mount a persistent volume to a fixed system path than a hidden dotfile path.

### Change Type
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Chore/infra

target.title: fix(cron): prevent duplicate job execution after gateway restart (#42640)
target.body:
Fixes #42640

## Summary

After gateway restart, the cron scheduler re-executed jobs that already ran before the restart, causing duplicate deliveries. The root cause: `isRunnableJob` treated stale `nextRunAtMs <= now` slots as due without checking whether they had already been executed.

## Changes

- Add `hasAlreadyExecutedScheduledSlot` guard in `timer.ts`: if `lastRunAtMs >= nextRunAtMs`, the expired slot was already executed — skip it
- Preserves missed-slot fallthrough so genuinely missed jobs still get caught up

## Testing

- Added regression test: `does not re-run an already-executed stale due slot on restart`
- 501 tests pass across cron module

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",
  "hasRawKey": true,
  "exactLookupFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw"
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

Observed result after fix: The current prompt is 512 chars, below the 520 char cap, includes `<omitted_skill_keys>`, preserves the raw advertised key as JSON data, and `formatSkillInfo()` resolves the first advertised key exactly as `skill-00<zero`raw`.

What was not tested: Live model submission was not needed for this prompt-rendering fix; the local proof covers the real prompt builder and CLI exact lookup formatter path.

Local script generated 80 workspace skills with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520`, injected the raw omitted lookup key `skill-00<zero`raw`, and rendered the same prompt path through `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()`. The after case also passes the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup.

target.title: feat(pairing): add configurable pairingMessage text per channel (#41058)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `pairingMessage` config to iMessage and Telegram channel configs, allowing operators to customize or suppress OpenClaw branding in pairing challenge messages sent to unknown senders.

Fixes #41058

## Problem

When `dmPolicy: pairing` is set, every unknown sender receives a hardcoded message exposing:
1. The OpenClaw brand name
2. The channel platform ("Your iMessage sender id:" / "Your Telegram user id:")
3. The full CLI invocation syntax (`openclaw pairing approve imessage CODE`)

No config option exists to override any part of this. The only workaround is patching compiled dist bundles after every update — not sustainable for white-label or privacy-conscious deployments.

## What changed

- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfig` type with optional fields (`header`, `senderIdLabel`, `codeLabel`, `footer`, `showCliHint`). Update `buildPairingReply` to use config when provided.
- `src/config/types.imessage.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfigSchema`, wire into `IMessageAccountSchemaBase` and `TelegramAccountSchemaBase`
- `src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts`: Wire `pairingMessage` config into `issuePairingChallenge` via existing `buildReplyText` hook
- `src/telegram/dm-access.ts`: Pass `pairingMessage` through `enforceTelegramDmAccess`, wire into `issuePairingChallenge`
- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.test.ts`: Add tests for all config overrides and backward compat

## Fully backward compatible

All `PairingMessageConfig` fields are optional. Existing deployments with no `pairingMessage` config see zero behavior change.

## Example config

```yaml
channels:
  imessage:
    pairingMessage:
      header: 'Sender verification required.'
      senderIdLabel: 'Your sender ID:'
      footer: 'Share this code with your contact to complete verification.'
      showCliHint: false
```

## Notes

Discord, Signal, Slack, and LINE call sites follow the same pattern and can be extended in a follow-up if the team prefers to land iMessage/Telegram first.

## AI Disclosure
This PR was authored with AI assistance (OpenClaw/Jordan + Neo agents).
Testing: Fully tested — unit tests in `pairing-messages.test.ts` cover all config branches including edge cases; CI passes (types, lint, full test suite on Linux/Windows/macOS).
The authors have reviewed and understand all code changes in this PR.

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress NO_REPLY sentinel before sendMessageTelegram API call
target.body:
## Summary

- `sendMessageSlack` guards against `NO_REPLY` sentinel tokens before calling the Slack API
- `sendMessageTelegram` was missing the same guard — a `NO_REPLY` token could reach the Telegram API
- This caused a visible flash of text (truncated to "NO") that Telegram briefly showed and then deleted once the stream handler suppressed the sentinel

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #38603

## Root Cause

When an agent outputs visible narration text before the `NO_REPLY` sentinel in the same response, the text streams to Telegram as a live preview. OpenClaw's stream handler correctly suppresses `NO_REPLY` at the end, but the preview was already delivered. `sendMessageSlack` has a defensive guard that intercepts `NO_REPLY` tokens before any API call; `sendMessageTelegram` lacked this guard.

## Fix

Added `isSilentReplyText` check at the entry of `sendMessageTelegram`, matching the existing Slack pattern. Returns a suppressed no-op result without making a network call when the text is a `NO_REPLY` sentinel and no media is attached.

target.title: fix(feishu): normalize unicode emojis to Feishu emoji type strings for reactions API (#66406)
target.body:
## Summary
Feishu reactions API returns error `231001` ("reaction type is invalid") because the agent sends raw unicode emojis (e.g. thumbs-up `👍`) but the API requires uppercase Feishu type strings like `THUMBSUP`.

## Root Cause
In `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`, the `emoji` parameter from the tool call is passed directly to `addReactionFeishu()` without conversion. The Feishu API only accepts its own emoji type vocabulary (e.g. `THUMBSUP`, `HEART`, `FIRE`) per https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce, not unicode characters.

ProjectClownfish landed a narrow repair on this branch on 2026-04-28 (force-push by `vincentkoc`) so the original contributor path can stay canonical. The fix shape and tests have been stable since then.

## Changes
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`: Add `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` function with a unicode-to-Feishu-type mapping table covering all common emoji reactions. Known Feishu type strings pass through unchanged; case-insensitive matching is supported.
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Apply `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` to the emoji parameter before calling `addReactionFeishu()` and `listReactionsFeishu()`.
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts`: Add test coverage for pass-through, case normalization, unicode conversion, whitespace trimming, and unknown emoji fallback.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Tool calls that pass unicode emoji to `addReactionFeishu` send `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "👍" } }` to the Feishu reactions HTTP API, which rejects it with `code: 231001` and `msg: "reaction type is invalid"`. After the fix the same tool call sends `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "THUMBSUP" } }`, which is the documented Feishu emoji type vocabulary. Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-66406` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. Because the worktree's `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` imports run-time Feishu SDK modules transitively, the smoke script extracts the **production `FeishuEmoji` constant table and `normalizeFeishuEmoji` function body verbatim** from `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` (SHA-256-pinned for integrity) and executes that exact slice via `node --experimental-strip-types`. No mocks, no test runner — the live function body is what runs. PR head: `a3bebf502f1ce0ebfe3b754ec1ab67b3302098fc`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/feishu-reaction-emoji-type && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts`. The smoke script reads `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`, slices from `export const FeishuEmoji` to end-of-file, prints the prod-slice SHA-256, then exercises `normalizeFeishuEmoji` against the actual reporter unicode emojis.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3bebf502f` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, 2245-byte production slice verified by SHA-256 `8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts
$ wc -c C:\...\feishu-combined-1778492490068.mts
  bytes: 3667
(prod slice: 2245 bytes copied verbatim from extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts starting at offset 3989)
(SHA-256 of prod slice for integrity:)
  8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54

=== Sample known Feishu emoji constants ===
FeishuEmoji.THUMBSUP = THUMBSUP
FeishuEmoji.HEART    = HEART
FeishuEmoji.CRY      = CRY

=== Unicode emoji -> Feishu type name ===
  "👍"             (thumbs up)  ->  THUMBSUP
  "❤️"             (red heart)  ->  HEART
  "😂"             (face with tears of joy)  ->  LAUGHING
  "🎉"             (party popper)  ->  PARTY
  "🙏"             (folded hands)  ->  PRAY
  "🔥"             (fire)  ->  FIRE

=== Bypass cases (must NOT be touched) ===
  "THUMBSUP"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "thumbsup"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "ThumbsUp"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "  HEART  "     -> "HEART"
  "UNKNOWN_TYPE"  -> "UNKNOWN_TYPE"

=== Request body produced for issue #66406 reproducer ===
user sends: "👍"
request body: {"reaction":{"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"}}
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: Each tested unicode emoji is mapped to a documented Feishu type name (`👍`→`THUMBSUP`, `❤️`→`HEART`, `😂`→`LAUGHING`, `🎉`→`PARTY`, `🙏`→`PRAY`, `🔥`→`FIRE`) — exactly the vocabulary listed at https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce. Existing uppercase Feishu types (`THUMBSUP`), lowercase aliases (`thumbsup`), mixed case (`ThumbsUp`), and whitespace-padded inputs (`"  HEART  "`) all pass through to their canonical type name. Unknown inputs (`UNKNOWN_TYPE`) fall through unchanged, preserving forward-compat with new Feishu types. The final reproducer line confirms the runtime request body now contains `"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"` — which is exactly what the Feishu API requires to avoid `code: 231001`.
- **What was not tested**: A live `addReactionFeishu` HTTP roundtrip to the actual Feishu Open Platform server with a paired bot token + chat_id is not exercised on the contributor machine (would need a configured Feishu workspace). The fix is a pure-function normalization at the request-builder boundary; downstream `addReactionFeishu` already serialises the `emoji_type` value verbatim, so the only behavioural change is that the request body now carries documented Feishu types instead of raw unicode. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live Feishu API recording is required; vincentkoc / ProjectClownfish already validated this fix shape via `pnpm -s vitest run extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` and the gated `pnpm check:changed` lanes when force-pushing on 2026-04-28.

## Test

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` — 5 cases (pass-through / case normalization / unicode conversion / whitespace trimming / unknown emoji fallback)
- `pnpm check:changed` — green (per vincentkoc's 2026-04-28 status update)

Closes #66406

target.title: Browser: harden noVNC bootstrap headers
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the loopback-only `/sandbox/novnc` bootstrap page relied on `no-store` and `no-referrer`, but it did not send a document CSP, `nosniff`, or frame-deny headers.

- What did NOT change (scope boundary): the existing one-time observer token URL flow and noVNC redirect behavior stay the same.

target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: sessions with `authProfileOverride=provider:default` could remain pinned to the legacy default id even when a canonical oauth alias (for example `provider:user@example.com`) existed.
- Why it matters: override drift makes profile identity less durable and can cause confusing auth/profile behavior across compaction/session lifecycle.
- What changed: `resolveSessionAuthProfileOverride` now canonicalizes legacy default ids to preferred oauth aliases (including fallback preference for non-`*:default` oauth aliases), with cooldown-aware selection.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no transport/auth provider protocol changes, no new permissions, no config schema changes.

target.title: fix(msteams): delete FileConsentCard after user accepts, declines, or upload expires
target.body:
## Problem

The `FileConsentCard` (Allow/Decline prompt for file uploads) remains in the Teams chat after the user acts on it, cluttering the conversation with stale interactive cards. Users see orphaned Allow/Decline buttons even after files have been successfully uploaded or the upload has expired.

## Solution

Call `context.deleteActivity(activity.replyToId)` to remove the original consent card after processing. The invoke activity from Bot Framework includes the consent card's activity ID in `replyToId`.

Deletion is applied on all four code paths in `handleFileConsentInvoke`:

| Path | Before | After |
|------|--------|-------|
| User accepts, upload succeeds | Consent card remains | Deleted, only FileInfoCard shown |
| User declines | Consent card remains | Deleted |
| Pending upload expired (not found) | Consent card remains, expired message sent | Deleted, expired message sent |
| Conversation mismatch (security) | Consent card remains | Deleted |

Implemented as a best-effort `tryDeleteConsentCard()` helper — if deletion fails (e.g. permissions, timing), the upload flow continues normally with a debug log.

Per [Microsoft docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoftteams/platform/bots/how-to/bots-filesv4):
> *Optionally, remove the original consent card if you do not want the user to accept further uploads of the same file.*

## Testing

- Added 4 new tests covering consent card deletion on all paths
- Updated existing test harness to include `deleteActivity` mock and `replyToId` field
- Verified in production Teams environment (NemoClaw deployment)

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Clawdbot on OpenClaw). The bug was identified during real-world testing of file uploads in a Teams bot deployment.

target.title: fix(signal): forward quote metadata to agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Signal quoted replies (using the native reply/quote feature) arrive with `dataMessage.quote` containing `id`, `author`, and `text` from signal-cli, but only `quote.text` was being extracted — `id` and `author` were silently dropped, and none of the `ReplyTo*` context fields were populated in `finalizeInboundContext()`.

This means agents receiving Signal messages cannot tell what message a user was replying to, even though the data is available. The reply text itself arrives fine, but the quote context (which message was being replied to, who wrote it) is lost.

Telegram already handles this correctly via `describeReplyTarget()` which populates `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, and `ReplyToIsQuote`. This PR mirrors that behavior for Signal.

## Changes

- **`event-handler.types.ts`**: Expand `SignalDataMessage.quote` type to include `id`, `author`, `authorNumber` fields (matching signal-cli JSON-RPC protocol)
- **`event-handler.ts`**: 
  - Add `signalQuote` field to `SignalInboundEntry` type
  - Extract quote metadata from `dataMessage.quote` before debouncer enqueue
  - Set `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, `ReplyToIsQuote` in inbound context payload

## Before / After

**Before:** Agent receives `Body: "yes that sounds good"` — no indication this was a reply to a specific message.

**After:** Agent receives:
- `Body: "yes that sounds good"` 
- `ReplyToId: "1774743992672"` (timestamp of quoted message)
- `ReplyToBody: "Should we deploy the fix tonight?"` (original message text)
- `ReplyToSender: "+1234567890"` (who wrote the original)
- `ReplyToIsQuote: true`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send a normal (non-reply) Signal message → verify no ReplyTo fields set
- [ ] Send a quoted reply → verify ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToSender, ReplyToIsQuote populated
- [ ] Send a reply with no new text (quote-only) → verify bodyText falls back to quote text, ReplyTo fields still set
- [ ] Send a message in a group with a quote → verify same behavior in group context
- [ ] Verify Telegram reply behavior unchanged

target.title: fix: route embedded tool calls through in-process dispatch (#40237)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #40237 (also resolves #5703 and #6508 which were circular-duped shut).

When agent tools like `cron.run`, `cron.list`, `cron.add` are called from within an active LLM session, `callGatewayTool` opens a **new WebSocket connection** to the same gateway. Because the gateway's Node.js event loop is busy processing the current LLM turn, the second WS connection sits in queue and times out after 10s.

This PR routes embedded tool calls through the existing in-process dispatch path (`dispatchGatewayMethod`) when running inside the gateway process, bypassing WS entirely. External CLI tool calls continue to use WS unchanged.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/local-dispatch.ts`** (new): lightweight registry that the gateway server populates at startup. Exposes `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` which returns a promise when in-process dispatch is available, or `undefined` when running outside the gateway (e.g. CLI).

- **`src/gateway/server-plugins.ts`**: registers `dispatchGatewayMethod` as the local dispatcher when `setFallbackGatewayContext` is called during gateway startup.

- **`src/agents/tools/gateway.ts`**: `callGatewayTool` now checks `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` first. If available (inside gateway), dispatches in-process with zero WS overhead. Otherwise falls through to the existing WS path.

## How it works

```
CLI tool call (external):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns undefined → WS path (unchanged)

Embedded tool call (inside gateway LLM session):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns Promise → dispatchGatewayMethod
    → handleGatewayRequest (in-process, no WS) → immediate response
```

The `dispatchGatewayMethod` function already exists and is battle-tested — it's used by plugin subagent runtime for sessions, agent dispatch, etc. This PR simply makes it available to agent tools via the registry pattern.

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests for `local-dispatch.ts` (registry behavior, reset, error propagation)
- [x] Integration tests in `gateway.test.ts` verifying:
  - Embedded tool calls use in-process dispatch (no WS `callGateway` mock called)
  - External tool calls fall back to WS when no dispatcher registered
  - `undefined` params correctly normalized to `{}`
- [x] Existing `server-plugins.test.ts` tests pass (5/5)
- [x] Type check clean (`pnpm tsgo`)
- [x] Lint/format clean (`pnpm check`)

target.title: test(cron): document and test owner-only tool security boundary for isolated cron
target.body:
## Summary

Document and test the cron owner-only tool security boundary in `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()`.

Isolated cron runs are executed with `senderIsOwner: false`. The `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()` function determines which owner-only tools are granted to the cron agent. Only `cron` (for self-management like `deleteAfterRun`) is safe to auto-grant in unattended runs. Control-plane tools (`gateway`, `nodes`) must not be auto-granted.

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-executor.ts` | Extract `CRON_SAFE_OWNER_ONLY_TOOLS` constant, export function, use `isOwnerOnlyToolName()` + safe-list filter |
| `run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts` | 6 test cases covering the security boundary |

### What this does NOT fix

Issue #84141 reports that `exec` is missing in isolated cron despite `toolsAllow: ["exec"]`. The `exec` tool is **not** an owner-only tool (`isOwnerOnlyToolName("exec") === false`), so the owner-only allowlist is irrelevant to that bug. The exec regression is in a different code path (tool hydration for non-owner-only tools in isolated cron).

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (6 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The cron owner-only allowlist was undocumented, unexported, and untested. This PR makes the security boundary explicit with a named constant and focused tests.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist` function from the source tree.

### Owner-only tool classification (tool-policy.ts)

```
cron:    isOwnerOnly=true   ← only this is cron-safe
gateway: isOwnerOnly=true   ← control-plane, NOT cron-safe
nodes:   isOwnerOnly=true   ← exec-capable, NOT cron-safe
exec:    isOwnerOnly=false  ← NOT owner-only at all (#84141 is unrelated)
```

### Security boundary proof

```
✅ toolsAllow=undefined              → undefined (no grants)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","read","write"] → undefined (no owner-only tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","cron","nodes"] → ["cron"]  (nodes filtered out)
✅ toolsAllow=["gateway","nodes"]     → undefined (no cron-safe tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron"]                → ["cron"]  (cron granted)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron","gateway","nodes"] → ["cron"] (gateway+nodes filtered)
```

6/6 cases pass. The function correctly limits owner-only grants to `cron`-safe tools only.

- **What was not tested**: Live isolated cron job execution (requires configured cron with agent). The function-level proof validates the security boundary. Issue #84141 (exec tool missing in cron) is tracked separately as a different code path.

target.title: Add time-based session maintenance modes
target.body:
## Summary
- add time-based session maintenance settings using `sessionCacheTtl` plus `timeBasedContextCompact`
- support explicit `compact` and `reset` maintenance modes instead of implicitly coupling both behaviors together
- track session idle/cache timestamps and run gateway maintenance sweeps to apply the selected policy

## Why
This change adds two time-based session maintenance workflows for long-lived sessions:

1. `compact`
   - Purpose: reduce the chance of a cold cache miss after a long idle period by proactively compacting an idle session shortly before the configured timeout.
   - Good fit: long research or coding threads where the user may come back later and continue from the same context.

2. `reset`
   - Purpose: aggressively clear stale sessions once they have been idle past the configured timeout instead of carrying old context forward.
   - Good fit: workflows where stale context is more harmful than helpful, or where keeping expired sessions around only increases cost and surprise.

The new behavior is controlled explicitly per model:
- `sessionCacheTtl` defines the timeout window
- `timeBasedContextCompact` chooses the maintenance algorithm: `none`, `compact`, or `reset`

`cacheRetention` remains a model prompt-cache setting and is no longer used to enable or disable these new maintenance features.

## Implementation notes
- keep Anthropic API-key smart defaults at `cacheRetention: "short"`
- add `sessionCacheTtl: "1h"` as a defaulted timeout value without auto-enabling maintenance mode
- warn when `sessionCacheTtl` exceeds an explicitly configured model cache TTL
- record `lastUserMessageAt`, `lastAssistantMessageAt`, `lastCacheTouchAt`, and `lastIdleCompactionForCacheTouchAt` in session state
- run the maintenance sweep from gateway server maintenance without overlapping runs

## Validation

target.title: fix(discord): preserve mentionedUsers on debounced synthetic messages
target.body:
## Summary

- When multiple Discord messages are debounced (e.g. chunked bot replies), the synthetic message created via object spread (`{ ...last.data.message }`) loses Carbon `Message` prototype getters (`mentionedUsers`, `mentionedRoles`, `mentionedEveryone`)
- This causes bot-to-bot @mention detection to always fail for multi-chunk messages, since `explicitlyMentioned` evaluates to `false` on the plain object
- Fix: materialise mention data from all debounced entries into the synthetic message object

## Root cause

`src/discord/monitor/message-handler.ts` creates a synthetic message when debouncing multiple inbound messages into one:

```ts
const syntheticMessage = {
  ...last.data.message,   // ← Carbon Message instance; spread copies own props only
  content: combinedBaseText,
  // mentionedUsers getter lives on the prototype → lost after spread
};
```

In `preflightDiscordMessage`, `message.mentionedUsers` returns `undefined` instead of the mention array, so:
1. `explicitlyMentioned = Boolean(botId && message.mentionedUsers?.some(...))` → `false`
2. Text-based regex also fails because `resolveDiscordMentions` already rewrote `<@ID>` → `@name` in the combined text
3. Result: "no-mention" skip for every bot-to-bot @mention in chunked messages

User-to-bot mentions work because single messages (`entries.length === 1`) pass through the original Carbon Message instance.

## Test plan

- [ ] Bot A sends a multi-chunk message mentioning Bot B → Bot B should now detect the mention and respond
- [ ] Single-message mentions (user→bot, bot→bot) continue to work
- [ ] Messages with no mentions still get "no-mention" skip as expected

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(models): expose provider rate-limit headroom in probe results
target.body:
## Summary

Adds rate-limit headroom information to `openclaw models status --probe` output. When `--rate-limits` is passed, each successful probe result includes remaining RPM/TPM data extracted from provider HTTP response headers.

Closes #42401

## Changes

### New types (`list.types.ts`)
- `RateLimitInfo` — structured rate-limit data (remaining/limit for requests and tokens, reset timestamps)

### Probe logic (`list.probe.ts`)
- `parseIntHeader()` — safely parse numeric header values
- `parseRateLimitHeaders()` — extract `x-ratelimit-*` headers into `RateLimitInfo`
- `buildProviderEndpoint()` — build minimal 1-token API requests per provider (Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible, Google). Respects custom `baseUrl` from `models.providers` config.
- `resolveApiKeyForTarget()` — resolve API key for a probe target (profile-based or env-based)
- `probeRateLimits()` — lightweight direct HTTP call to capture rate-limit headers. Only fires after the main probe succeeds. Best-effort: failures are silently ignored.
- `formatRateLimitShort()` — format remaining/limit as compact strings for CLI table
- `rateLimit` field added to `AuthProbeResult`
- `rateLimits` option threaded through `AuthProbeOptions` → `probeTarget` → `runTargetsWithConcurrency` → `runAuthProbes`

### CLI (`models-cli.ts`, `list.status-command.ts`)
- `--rate-limits` flag added to `models status`
- When set, the probe table includes RPM and TPM columns
- JSON output always includes `rateLimit` field (when `--rate-limits` is set)

### Tests (`list.probe.test.ts`)
- 13 new tests covering:
  - `parseIntHeader`: valid ints, null/undefined/empty, non-numeric, floats
  - `parseRateLimitHeaders`: full headers, no headers, partial headers, token-only, non-numeric values
  - `formatRateLimitShort`: null input, full pairs, remaining-only, limit-only, empty object

## Design Decisions

1. **Separate HTTP call** — Rather than modifying `runEmbeddedPiAgent` (core pipeline), rate-limit probing makes a separate lightweight `fetch()` call with `max_tokens=1`. This avoids touching the agent pipeline and keeps the change isolated.

2. **Opt-in** — The `--rate-limits` flag is required. Without it, behavior is identical to before. The extra API call costs fractions of a cent but is still a real billable call.

3. **Custom base URLs** — `buildProviderEndpoint()` reads `models.providers.<provider>.baseUrl` from config, so custom endpoints (proxies, Azure, etc.) are respected.

4. **Provider coverage** — Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq, xAI, Cerebras, Mistral, OpenRouter, and Google are supported. Unknown providers return `null` (no rate-limit data) without error.

5. **Best-effort** — Rate-limit probing never fails the overall probe. All errors are caught silently.

## Usage

```bash
# CLI table with rate limits
openclaw models status --probe --rate-limits

# JSON output (for scripting/automation)
openclaw models status --probe --rate-limits --json
```

## Example output

```
Auth probes
Model                    Profile              Status        RPM Remaining  TPM Remaining
anthropic/claude-opus-4-6  anthropic:key        ok · 1.2s     945/1000       78000/80000
openai/gpt-4o            env                  ok · 0.8s     4500/5000      890000/1000000
google/gemini-1.5-flash  env                  ok · 0.5s     -              -
```

target.title: fix(hooks): load workspace hooks for non-default agents
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes #42072 by loading workspace-local hooks for every configured agent workspace at gateway startup, without changing the global hook registry model.

## Root cause
Gateway startup only called `loadInternalHooks(cfg, defaultWorkspaceDir)` once, so only the default workspace contributed `<workspace>/hooks/` entries to the global registry.

## Changes
- added a startup-specific multi-workspace loader in `src/hooks/loader.ts`
- loaded shared hooks once and workspace-local hooks once per actual workspace hooks directory
- wrapped workspace-local handlers with workspace-aware scope guards based on event workspace, agent, or session
- preserved legacy handler loading relative to the default workspace
- updated `src/gateway/server-startup.ts` to enumerate all agent workspaces via `listAgentWorkspaceDirs`

## Validation
- added regression coverage for:
  - multi-workspace loading
  - scope isolation
  - shared-hook dedupe
  - shared/local precedence
  - `gateway:startup` behavior
- passed:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/hooks/loader.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`

## Notes
- this keeps the existing global internal hook registry unchanged
- this PR avoids broad event-emitter or registry refactors

target.title: (googlechat) fix: space messaging, introduce deduplication, and update reply-to defaults
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- **Problem:** Google Chat messages in Spaces were not handling threads/reply-to correctly, and duplicate events from Google's webhook often caused redundant bot responses.
- **Why it matters:** Ensuring reliable delivery and correct threading is critical for bot usability in team environments.
- **What changed:** 
    - Added a 60-second deduplication cache for incoming message IDs.
    - Updated default `replyToMode` for Google Chat from `off` to [all](cci:1://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:331:6-332:80) in [src/channels/dock.ts](cci:7://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:0:0-0:0).
    - Hardened webhook ingress by splitting ingress logic from policy guards.
    - Added support for `ADDED_TO_SPACE` events.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to authentication flow or other channel plugins.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related # (Please link the original issue if applicable)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **Default Setting:** `replyToMode` for Google Chat now defaults to [all](cci:1://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:331:6-332:80) instead of `off`.
- **Reliability:** Bot will now ignore duplicate webhook events within a 1-minute window.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat extension

### Steps

1. Add the bot to a Google Chat Space.
2. Send a message to the bot.
3. Observe if the bot replies in the same thread.
4. Simulate duplicate webhook triggers to verify deduplication.

### Expected

- Bot replies correctly in-thread.
- Each unique message triggers exactly one bot response.

### Actual

- Bot replies in-thread and ignores duplicate events.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets (Verified `recentlyProcessedMessages` cache behavior locally).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Tagging bot in Spaces, Direct Messages, and handling of duplicate webhook payloads.
- Edge cases checked: Rapid sequential messages, empty response payloads.
- What you did **not** verify: High-volume traffic (exceeding 1000 msgs/min).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`None`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the changes in [src/channels/dock.ts](cci:7://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:0:0-0:0) and `extensions/googlechat/src/`.
- Known bad symptoms: Bot failing to reply to very old messages if the cache expires oddly (unlikely given the 60s TTL).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The 60s deduplication cache uses memory (Map).
  - Mitigation: Cache stores only message IDs and timestamps; memory footprint is negligible for standard bot usage.
